      "description": "Add v0.42 changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.42 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-42\">v0.42 &mdash; SVG flipY default fixed, flipX added</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>flipY default changed from <code>true</code> to <code>false</code>.</strong> Standard SVG (Illustrator, Inkscape, Figma, web exports) uses Y-down, same as Canvas and CSS. Mirroring Y on a Y-down SVG produces an upside-down result &mdash; which is exactly what v0.28..v0.41's default did. The original comment claimed \"most consumer SVGs are Y-down so we flip them,\" which is contradictory: if they're already Y-down, no flip should be needed for them to appear right-side-up. v0.42 reverts to the natural default. Standard SVGs now trace correctly without any options.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>New <code>flipX</code> option (default false).</strong> Analogous to <code>flipY</code> but for horizontal mirroring. Useful when an SVG appears horizontally mirrored &mdash; most commonly because a parent <code>&lt;g&gt;</code> has a <code>transform=\"scale(-1,1)\"</code> that <code>pathStringFromSvgElement</code> doesn't apply. Pass <code>flipX: true</code> to mirror at trace time without editing the SVG.</li>\n          <li><strong>Migration:</strong>\n            <ul>\n              <li>If you were passing <code>flipY: false</code> explicitly to fix upside-down output &mdash; you can now omit it. Code continues to work; the option is just redundant.</li>\n              <li>If you were not passing <code>flipY</code> and your SVGs looked correct &mdash; you were using one of the rare Y-up sources (older CAD/plot exports). To preserve behavior, pass <code>flipY: true</code> at your call sites.</li>\n              <li>If you were not passing <code>flipY</code> and your SVGs looked upside-down &mdash; v0.42 fixes that without code changes on your end. This is the common case.</li>\n            </ul>\n          </li>\n          <li>Pure data fix: no API surface changes beyond the new option. Same one-liner in the loader; defaults inverted.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.41 ===== -->",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.42 shipped.** Two things, with a fair bit of \"wait, why was it like that in the first place?\" attached.\n\n## The bug, and why it was a bug\n\nThe v0.28 code had this comment defending `flipY: true` as the default:\n\n> Most consumer SVGs (Illustrator, Inkscape, Figma, web design) are already Y-down. Flipping vertically turned out to be the more common desired behavior on real-world hand-drawn SVGs, so it's the default.\n\nThat logic doesn't survive even a second of scrutiny: **if standard SVGs are Y-down, and the rendering target (Canvas) is also Y-down, then no flip should be needed for them to render right-side-up.** Mirroring Y on a Y-down source flips it to upside-down. The comment contradicts the math.\n\nI verified with a simple test SVG: a curve from `(10,80)` to `(50,20)` to `(90,80)` \u2014 a hill peaking near the top in standard SVG convention. With `flipY: true`, those points became `(10,20)` \u2192 `(50,80)` \u2192 `(90,20)`, which is the same shape inverted (a U pointing down). So yes \u2014 the default was broken, every standard SVG looked upside-down out of the box.\n\nI'm not sure how this survived from v0.28 through v0.41. My best guess: the demos in the main app's wiring code pass `flipY` explicitly somewhere, masking the bad default for the dev iteration loop. Anyone calling `traceSVG()` from outside the demo with no options got the broken behavior.\n\n## What changed in v0.42\n\nThe defaults:\n\n```js\nflipY: false   // was true (v0.28..v0.41)\nflipX: false   // new in v0.42\n```\n\nThe math:\n\n```js\nx: offsetX + (flipX ? (vbW - (pt.x - vbX)) : (pt.x - vbX)) * s,\ny: offsetY + (flipY ? (vbH - (pt.y - vbY)) : (pt.y - vbY)) * s,\n```\n\n`flipX` is the same trick on the horizontal axis \u2014 mirror around the viewBox's horizontal center. Useful if an SVG appears horizontally flipped, typically because a parent `<g>` element has `transform=\"scale(-1,1)\"` that the path-string extractor doesn't apply.\n\n## Migration\n\nThree cases, depending on what you were doing before:\n\n1. **You were passing `flipY: false` explicitly** to compensate for upside-down output \u2192 you can now drop that option. Your code keeps working; the line is just redundant.\n\n2. **You weren't passing anything and SVGs looked correct** \u2192 you were using one of the rare genuinely Y-up SVG sources (older CAD, plot-style exports). To preserve behavior, pass `flipY: true` at your call sites. This case is uncommon but real.\n\n3. **You weren't passing anything and SVGs looked upside-down** \u2192 this is the common case. v0.42 fixes it for free. No code changes needed on your end.\n\n## Docs updated\n\n- **api-text-svg** \u2014 `flipY` doc reflects new default, added `flipX` entry with the v0.42 tag\n- **agents-signatures** \u2014 `SVGTraceOptions` interface updated with new defaults and `flipX`; also picked up `colorMap` while I was there\n- **agents-recipes** \u2014 SVG trace example no longer passes `flipY: true` explicitly; comment explains the default\n- **agents-invariants** \u2014 \"SVG trace flipY default\" entry rewritten to reflect v0.42 behavior, with explicit migration notes for all three cases above\n- **Footer** \u2014 v0.42 mention added\n\n## On the \"flipped along vertical axis\" part\n\nI added `flipX` as a real option because you mentioned it explicitly. The fix to `flipY` default should resolve the upside-down half. If you're still seeing horizontal mirroring after the flipY fix, pass `flipX: true` \u2014 and let me know what the source SVG looks like, because the current code doesn't introduce X mirroring anywhere on its own. Most likely cause if you're hitting it: a `transform` attribute on a parent group that isn't being honored, in which case `flipX: true` is the right band-aid and \"honor SVG transforms properly\" would be a separate, larger fix.",
    "citations": []
  }

If both are enabled, trigger colors should take priority since they're explicit matches, with approximation as a fallback for unrecognized colors. This gives us a clear precedence order across all four possible combinations of the flags.\n\nThe real challenge is figuring out how to approximate an arbitrary color as a mix of three pigments\u2014essentially solving the inverse Kubelka-Munk problem. I'm considering different approaches, starting with a simple RGB-based method. Looking at the pigment absorption and reflection characteristics, I need to verify my K/S values actually produce the expected colors when run through the Kubelka-Munk reflectance formula. Let me compute what the pure pigments should look like individually to make sure my parameters are sensible before attempting the mixing inversion. rose:\n  K/S = [0.22/0.05, 1.47/0.003, 0.57/0.03] = [4.4, 490, 19]\n  R = [1+4.4-sqrt(4.4^2+2*4.4), 1+490-sqrt(490^2+980), 1+19-sqrt(19^2+38)]\n    = [1+4.4-sqrt(28.16), 1+490-sqrt(240080), 1+19-sqrt(399)]\n    = [5.4-5.31, 491-490.0, 20-19.97]\n    = [0.09, 0.99... wait that gives R_green=0.99 for rose? That can't be right]\n\nHmm, that doesn't match my intuition for rose. Let me recheck.\n\nActually, when K/S is very large like 490, the square root term dominates and nearly cancels out the K/S term, leaving R close to zero. For the more modest K/S values, I get small but non-zero reflectance values around 0.09 and 0.19.\n\nThe key insight is that pure pigment reflectance is quite dark, but in the actual simulation, the rose color appears pink because the pigment layer is thin\u2014the underlying paper reflects through, blending with the pigment's reflectance to create the lighter, more saturated pink we see visually.\n\nFor the approximate color feature, I should stop trying to invert the Kubelka-Munk equations and instead use a simpler approach: find the closest match in CIE Lab space to a palette of mixed pigment swatches that the simulation can actually produce. Even better, I can leverage color theory directly\u2014since the three pigments roughly map to red, yellow, and blue, I can let users think intuitively about mixing: orange is red plus yellow, green is yellow plus blue, purple is red plus blue, and so on.\n\nThis is essentially subtractive RGB-to-RYB conversion, which has established approximations. I could either convert RGB to RYB through a non-linear remapping and use those values as mix proportions, or work backwards from CMY complementary values and map them to how each pigment absorbs light at different wavelengths.\n\nThe second approach maps Cyan to Blue, Magenta to Rose, and Yellow to Yellow, then normalizes those weights. Let me test this against some basic colors to see if it holds up. ue + Rose, no Yellow \u2192 blue-purple. Hmm.\n- Cyan (#00FFFF) \u2192 CMY = (1, 0, 0) \u2192 all Blue \u2192 cyan. Yes!\n- Magenta (#FF00FF) \u2192 CMY = (0, 1, 0) \u2192 all Rose \u2192 magenta. Yes!\n- Yellow (#FFFF00) \u2192 CMY = (0, 0, 1) \u2192 all Yellow \u2192 yellow. Yes!\n- Orange (#FF8000) \u2192 CMY = (0, 0.5, 1) \u2192 Rose + heavy Yellow \u2192 orange-yellow. Yes!\n- Purple (#8000FF) \u2192 CMY = (0.5, 1, 0) \u2192 light Blue + heavy Rose \u2192 purple. Yes!\n\nPure blue reading as \"blue + rose\" makes sense for saturation \u2014 mixing in rose gives it more depth, which aligns with how watercolor actually works. So I can convert RGB to CMY, then map those values to pigment weights. The challenge is that the painting library only accepts one pigment per call, so I'd need to either make multiple paint calls with different pigments or find another way to blend them on the canvas.\n\nI'm weighing two approaches: randomly selecting pigments weighted by the CMY proportions (which would let the compositor handle mixing naturally but might look noisy), or painting each point multiple times with each pigment at proportional strength (which would give smoother results since all pigments contribute to every point). The second approach seems better for avoiding visible spottiness in solid colors and gradients.\n\nFor the strength calculation, if I have weights like w_rose=0.5 and w_yellow=0.5, I'd paint rose at 0.5 strength and yellow at 0.5 strength using a base strength value, skipping any pigments with zero weight. This triples the paint calls but produces smooth colors. Now I'm realizing there's a subtlety with how multiple paint operations at partial strength actually combine...\n\nWhen I paint at 0.5 strength and then paint another pigment at 0.5 strength on the same point, the second stamp adds to the existing wet cell and the pigments mix together, which is exactly what I want for the K-M compositor to render the blended color. The risk is over-saturation from multiple stamps, but the simplest approach is to just call paintAt multiple times with proportional strengths and let the simulation handle any saturation naturally\u2014it's tunable if needed. Rather than modifying paintAt's signature to handle weights directly, I'll treat this as a multi-pigment extension where each pigment gets deposited proportionally.\n\nFor approximated points, I'll loop through the three pigments and call paintAt for each one with strength scaled by its weight. This means three times more paintAt calls for those points, but it produces correct color mixing through the compositor. I need to extend my point model to store multiple pigments instead of just a single one.\n\nNow I'm designing the resolver function that walks up the SVG tree to find stroke values and determine whether to use a single pigment or approximate the color...\n\nWriting resolver function... Still writing color parsing logic...\n\nI'm realizing the normalization approach breaks the brightness information\u2014pure red should map to mostly rose pigment, not an equal mix of rose and yellow. Black (0,0,0) converts to CMY (1,1,1), meaning heavy deposits of all three pigments to approximate black, while white (1,1,1) needs no pigment at all. Greys fall in between with moderate amounts of all three, creating that brownish-grey watercolor effect. The algorithm is shaping up correctly.\n\nNow I'm implementing the conversion function that maps RGB to pigment weights, then handling the painting phase where each point deposits pigment at strengths proportional to those weights.\n\nThe tricky part is managing wetness\u2014calling paintAt three times at the same location would triple the water and pressure, causing excessive bleeding. I could either have only the first call add water while subsequent ones add just pigment, or scale each call's strength by a third to keep the total brush load consistent. The cleanest approach is to think of paintAt strength as the amount of pigment to deposit. For mixed paint, I want each pigment i to receive S * w[i] / sum(w), where S is what a single normal stamp would deposit. This keeps the total pigment constant regardless of color\u2014pure red, black, and rose all deposit the same total amount. The real distinction is that pure red is fully opaque with maximum pigment, while light pink is the same pigment mixture but much more diluted.\n\nI'm weighing three approaches: accepting extra wetness for multi-pigment colors since that mirrors real watercolor behavior, normalizing and sacrificing brightness accuracy, or using the sum of weights as a saturation indicator to scale the base strength proportionally.\n\nOption C creates an odd problem where pure rose ends up dimmer than pure red, which doesn't feel right. An alternative would be to sample stochastically\u2014pick one pigment per point weighted by the mix ratios, then paint once. This keeps wetness normal and lets colors blend naturally through Kubelka-Munk, though it only works well if the path has enough sample points to average out correctly.\n\nThe tradeoff is between stochastic (smooth, correct wetness, but needs density) versus multi-stamp (deterministic proportions, but 2-3x wetter and slower). Actually, I should look at how the rainbow brush handles this\u2014it cycles through pigments over time. But more importantly, I'm realizing the K-M mixing happens in the compositor *after* the pigments are deposited, so if I put 0.5 rose and 0.5 yellow in the same cell, the render automatically outputs the mixed color.\n\nSo for a 50/50 blend, I can either stamp both pigments separately (double wetness) or use stochastic sampling to randomly pick one or the other weighted by probability\u2014over many sample points along a path, this averages to the right proportions with only single wetness. Stochastic is the way to go for v1: simpler, correct wetness, and works great with SVG paths that have plenty of sample points.\n\nImplementing the mix type with weighted random selection...\n\nWait, I need to think through the strength interaction more carefully. The stochastic approach picks which pigment to deposit, but the total pigment amount is still controlled by brush strength and size\u2014so that's independent of the color mixing logic. The weights just determine which pigment gets chosen at each point, not how much total pigment deposits.\n\nThe real issue is that with equal strength across all colors, grey and black end up depositing the same total pigment, just in different proportions. But black should be darker, meaning more total pigment. I need to scale the strength itself by the sum of the weights\u2014so lighter colors (lower weight sum) paint with less strength, and darker colors paint with more. ose: weight=(1,0,0), sum=1, strength=base*1 \u2192 normal strength rose\n- Pure red: weight=(1,1,0), sum=2 \u2014 hmm but we want red to be as saturated as rose. Red is rose AND yellow, so it's MORE total pigment per stroke. Black requires lots of pigment mixing.\n\nThat actually matches reality! When you paint red in watercolor, you load your brush with two pigments and you get more deposit. Black requires lots of pigment mixing.\n\nSo the total pigment scaling per sum(weights) IS correct watercolor behavior. I shouldn't normalize.\n\nFor the stochastic approach to give correct darkness, I need probability of \"no paint\" = 1 - sum(weights) / 3 when sum < 3, and if paint happens, the pigment gets chosen by weights.     "text": "Two related but distinct features. Let me think about the design before coding:\n\n**`triggerColors`** (renamed from `colorMap`) \u2014 keeps the strict chroma-key behavior. Just a better name since `colorMap` is vague.\n\n**`approximateColor`** \u2014 for any non-trigger color, decompose it into proportions of rose+yellow+blue based on subtractive color theory: each pigment absorbs its complementary wavelength.\n\n```\nrose   absorbs green \u2192 weight = 1 - g  (like magenta in CMYK)\nyellow absorbs blue  \u2192 weight = 1 - b  (like yellow in CMYK)\nblue   absorbs red   \u2192 weight = 1 - r  (like cyan in CMYK)\n```\n\nPure red `(1,0,0)` \u2192 weights `(1,1,0)` \u2192 rose + yellow \u2192 orange-red \u2713  \nPure green `(0,1,0)` \u2192 weights `(0,1,1)` \u2192 yellow + blue \u2192 green \u2713  \nPure black `(0,0,0)` \u2192 weights `(1,1,1)` \u2192 all three \u2192 dark muddy \u2713  \nPure white `(1,1,1)` \u2192 weights `(0,0,0)` \u2192 no paint \u2713\n\nFor deposition, each point gets one paintAt per non-zero weight, with strength scaled by weight.       "new_str": "// v0.41 \u2014 Map an SVG color string to a pigment index. Standard\n// \"chroma-key\" colors used by SVG authors to declare pigment per\n// element / per path; unrecognized colors return null so the caller\n// can fall back to opts.pigment or the active brush.\n//\n//   rose    = #FF0000 (pure red)         \u2192 0\n//   yellow  = #FFFF00 (pure yellow)      \u2192 1\n//   blue    = #0000FF (pure blue)        \u2192 2\n//   mask    = #00FF00 (pure green)       \u2192 MASK_INDEX\n//   rainbow = #FF00FF (pure magenta)     \u2192 RAINBOW_INDEX\n//\n// v0.43 \u2014 Refactored to share parsing with _parseSvgColor (the more\n// permissive parser used by the approximation path). Same strict\n// match semantics: only the exact pure primaries return a pigment\n// index; anything else returns null.\nfunction _colorToPigment(color) {\n  const rgb = _parseSvgColor(color);\n  if (rgb === null) return null;\n  // Round to 0..255 ints and check for the exact trigger primaries.\n  const r = Math.round(rgb[0] * 255);\n  const g = Math.round(rgb[1] * 255);\n  const b = Math.round(rgb[2] * 255);\n  if (r === 255 && g === 0   && b === 0  ) return 0;              // rose\n  if (r === 255 && g === 255 && b === 0  ) return 1;              // yellow\n  if (r === 0   && g === 0   && b === 255) return 2;              // blue\n  if (r === 0   && g === 255 && b === 0  ) return MASK_INDEX;     // mask\n  if (r === 255 && g === 0   && b === 255) return RAINBOW_INDEX;  // rainbow\n  return null;\n}\n\n// v0.43 \u2014 Parse a CSS/SVG color string into [r, g, b] components in\n// 0..1, or null if unparseable. Supports:\n//   - 3-digit hex:  #F00\n//   - 6-digit hex:  #FF0000\n//   - rgb(r,g,b):   absolute 0..255 OR percentages\n//   - rgba(...):    alpha ignored\n//   - named colors: a curated subset covering common usage\n// Returns null for 'none', 'transparent', empty, null, or unrecognized.\n//\n// Named colors omit the trigger set (red/yellow/blue/lime/magenta/\n// fuchsia) since those go through _colorToPigment for trigger handling\n// first. If approximation reaches here with one of those names, the\n// caller should already have intercepted it.\nfunction _parseSvgColor(c) {\n  if (!c || typeof c !== 'string') return null;\n  let s = c.trim().toLowerCase();\n  if (!s || s === 'none' || s === 'transparent' || s === 'currentcolor') {\n    return null;\n  }\n  // 3-digit hex\n  let m = s.match(/^#([0-9a-f])([0-9a-f])([0-9a-f])$/);\n  if (m) return [\n    parseInt(m[1] + m[1], 16) / 255,\n    parseInt(m[2] + m[2], 16) / 255,\n    parseInt(m[3] + m[3], 16) / 255,\n  ];\n  // 6-digit hex\n  m = s.match(/^#([0-9a-f]{2})([0-9a-f]{2})([0-9a-f]{2})$/);\n  if (m) return [\n    parseInt(m[1], 16) / 255,\n    parseInt(m[2], 16) / 255,\n    parseInt(m[3], 16) / 255,\n  ];\n  // rgb() or rgba() \u2014 absolute (0..255) or percentage; alpha ignored.\n  // Accepts both comma and whitespace-separated forms.\n  m = s.match(/^rgba?\\s*\\(\\s*([\\d.]+)\\s*(%?)[\\s,]+([\\d.]+)\\s*(%?)[\\s,]+([\\d.]+)\\s*(%?)(?:[\\s,]+[\\d.]+\\s*%?)?\\s*\\)$/);\n  if (m) {\n    const parse = (v, isPct) => {\n      const n = parseFloat(v);\n      if (!isFinite(n)) return null;\n      return isPct ? n / 100 : n / 255;\n    };\n    const r = parse(m[1], m[2] === '%');\n    const g = parse(m[3], m[4] === '%');\n    const b = parse(m[5], m[6] === '%');\n    if (r === null || g === null || b === null) return null;\n    return [\n      Math.max(0, Math.min(1, r)),\n      Math.max(0, Math.min(1, g)),\n      Math.max(0, Math.min(1, b)),\n    ];\n  }\n  // Named CSS colors (subset). Useful for SVGs authored in tools that\n  // emit names instead of hex. The CSS spec has ~140 names; this is\n  // the practical subset for hand-authored watercolor SVGs.\n  const named = {\n    red:        [1.000, 0.000, 0.000],\n    yellow:     [1.000, 1.000, 0.000],\n    blue:       [0.000, 0.000, 1.000],\n    green:      [0.000, 0.502, 0.000],\n    lime:       [0.000, 1.000, 0.000],\n    cyan:       [0.000, 1.000, 1.000],\n    aqua:       [0.000, 1.000, 1.000],\n    magenta:    [1.000, 0.000, 1.000],\n    fuchsia:    [1.000, 0.000, 1.000],\n    orange:     [1.000, 0.647, 0.000],\n    purple:     [0.502, 0.000, 0.502],\n    pink:       [1.000, 0.753, 0.796],\n    brown:      [0.647, 0.165, 0.165],\n    black:      [0.000, 0.000, 0.000],\n    white:      [1.000, 1.000, 1.000],\n    gray:       [0.502, 0.502, 0.502],\n    grey:       [0.502, 0.502, 0.502],\n    silver:     [0.753, 0.753, 0.753],\n    navy:       [0.000, 0.000, 0.502],\n    teal:       [0.000, 0.502, 0.502],\n    olive:      [0.502, 0.502, 0.000],\n    maroon:     [0.502, 0.000, 0.000],\n    indigo:     [0.294, 0.000, 0.510],\n    violet:     [0.933, 0.510, 0.933],\n    gold:       [1.000, 0.843, 0.000],\n    coral:      [1.000, 0.498, 0.314],\n    salmon:     [0.980, 0.502, 0.447],\n    khaki:      [0.941, 0.902, 0.549],\n    turquoise:  [0.251, 0.878, 0.816],\n    crimson:    [0.863, 0.078, 0.235],\n    skyblue:    [0.529, 0.808, 0.922],\n    lightblue:  [0.678, 0.847, 0.902],\n    lightgreen: [0.565, 0.933, 0.565],\n    darkgreen:  [0.000, 0.392, 0.000],\n    darkblue:   [0.000, 0.000, 0.545],\n    darkred:    [0.545, 0.000, 0.000],\n  };\n  if (s in named) return named[s];\n  return null;\n}\n\n// v0.43 \u2014 Decompose an RGB color into proportional weights of the\n// three watercolor pigments (rose, yellow, blue). Based on subtractive\n// color theory: each pigment absorbs its complementary wavelength.\n//   rose \u2248 magenta \u2192 absorbs green \u2192 weight = 1 - g\n//   yellow         \u2192 absorbs blue  \u2192 weight = 1 - b\n//   blue \u2248 cyan    \u2192 absorbs red   \u2192 weight = 1 - r\n// The result feeds the multi-stamp painter in svgTraceStep, where each\n// non-zero weight produces one paintAt call at strength * weight.\n// Brighter (closer to white) colors \u2192 smaller weights \u2192 less pigment.\n// Darker colors \u2192 larger weights \u2192 more pigment (and more wetness).\n// Pure white returns all zeros (no paint at all).\nfunction _rgbToPigmentMix(rgb) {\n  return [\n    Math.max(0, Math.min(1, 1 - rgb[1])),  // rose\n    Math.max(0, Math.min(1, 1 - rgb[2])),  // yellow\n    Math.max(0, Math.min(1, 1 - rgb[0])),  // blue\n  ];\n}",
      "old_str": "// v0.41 \u2014 Map an SVG color string to a pigment index. Standard\n// \"chroma-key\" colors used by SVG authors to declare pigment per\n// element / per path; unrecognized colors return null so the caller\n// can fall back to opts.pigment or the active brush.\n//\n//   rose    = #FF0000 (pure red)         \u2192 0\n//   yellow  = #FFFF00 (pure yellow)      \u2192 1\n//   blue    = #0000FF (pure blue)        \u2192 2\n//   mask    = #00FF00 (pure green)       \u2192 MASK_INDEX\n//   rainbow = #FF00FF (pure magenta)     \u2192 RAINBOW_INDEX\n//\n// Accepts 3-digit hex (#F00), 6-digit hex (#FF0000), and a handful of\n// named colors. Case-insensitive. Trims whitespace. Returns null for\n// 'none', 'transparent', and anything not in the recognized set.\nfunction _colorToPigment(color) {\n  if (!color || typeof color !== 'string') return null;\n  let c = color.trim().toLowerCase();\n  if (!c || c === 'none' || c === 'transparent') return null;\n  // Expand 3-digit hex to 6-digit\n  if (/^#[0-9a-f]{3}$/.test(c)) {\n    c = '#' + c[1] + c[1] + c[2] + c[2] + c[3] + c[3];\n  }\n  if (/^#[0-9a-f]{6}$/.test(c)) {\n    switch (c) {\n      case '#ff0000': return 0;              // rose\n      case '#ffff00': return 1;              // yellow\n      case '#0000ff': return 2;              // blue\n      case '#00ff00': return MASK_INDEX;     // mask\n      case '#ff00ff': return RAINBOW_INDEX;  // rainbow\n      default: return null;\n    }\n  }\n  // Named colors (CSS named colors that match the trigger hex values\n  // exactly; anything else returns null and falls through to fallback).\n  switch (c) {\n    case 'red':                  return 0;\n    case 'yellow':               return 1;\n    case 'blue':                 return 2;\n    case 'lime':                 return MASK_INDEX;\n    case 'magenta':\n    case 'fuchsia':              return RAINBOW_INDEX;\n    default:                     return null;\n  }\n}",

      "new_str": "// v0.41 / v0.43 \u2014 Walk up the SVG DOM tree from the geometry element\n// looking for a paint specification in stroke (preferred \u2014 most SVG\n// \"sketches\" use line drawings) or fill. Returns one of:\n//\n//   { type: 'single', pigment: number }   \u2014 exact trigger color matched\n//   { type: 'mix',    weights: [r, y, b] } \u2014 approximate-color mix\n//   { type: 'single', pigment: fallback }  \u2014 no recognized color found\n//\n// `triggerColors` (default true) enables the strict chroma-key lookup\n// for the five pure primaries. `approximateColor` (default false)\n// enables the CMY-based mix decomposition for any parseable color.\n// Both can be on simultaneously \u2014 triggers win for exact matches, then\n// approximation fills in for anything else.\nfunction _resolveSvgPaint(el, svgRoot, fallback, triggerColors, approximateColor) {\n  let cur = el;\n  for (let depth = 0; cur && cur !== svgRoot && depth < 32; depth++) {\n    // STROKE first \u2014 line-drawing SVGs primarily encode color in stroke\n    const stroke = _getSvgPresentationValue(cur, 'stroke');\n    if (stroke && stroke !== 'none' && stroke !== 'transparent') {\n      if (triggerColors) {\n        const pig = _colorToPigment(stroke);\n        if (pig !== null) return { type: 'single', pigment: pig };\n      }\n      if (approximateColor) {\n        const rgb = _parseSvgColor(stroke);\n        if (rgb !== null) {\n          return { type: 'mix', weights: _rgbToPigmentMix(rgb) };\n        }\n      }\n    }\n    // FILL second \u2014 filled-shape SVGs (logos, regions)\n    const fill = _getSvgPresentationValue(cur, 'fill');\n    if (fill && fill !== 'none' && fill !== 'transparent') {\n      if (triggerColors) {\n        const pig = _colorToPigment(fill);\n        if (pig !== null) return { type: 'single', pigment: pig };\n      }\n      if (approximateColor) {\n        const rgb = _parseSvgColor(fill);\n        if (rgb !== null) {\n          return { type: 'mix', weights: _rgbToPigmentMix(rgb) };\n        }\n      }\n    }\n    cur = cur.parentNode;\n  }\n  return { type: 'single', pigment: fallback };\n}",

      "description": "Add _paintSvgPoint helper for single + mix dispatch",
      "new_str": "function cancelSVGTrace() { svgTraceState = null; }\n\n// v0.43 \u2014 Per-point dispatch. Single-pigment paint \u2192 one paintAt call.\n// Mix paint \u2192 one paintAt per non-zero weight, each at strength scaled\n// by the weight. The simulation's KM compositor then renders the\n// combined deposited pigment as the approximated color naturally.\n//\n// Wetness consequence: a mix-paint point can fire 2-3 paintAt calls\n// at the same (x, y), which is 2-3\u00d7 the wetness of a single-pigment\n// stamp. That extra wetness causes more bleed for mid-saturation and\n// dark colors. This is actually accurate to real watercolor \u2014 mixing\n// two pigments physically does deposit more wet paint than using one.\n// If it's too much for a given use case, scale down brush strength\n// or waterLoad before calling traceSVG.\nfunction _paintSvgPoint(pt, st) {\n  const paint = pt.paint;\n  if (!paint) {\n    // Defensive fallback for points missing a paint descriptor\n    paintAt(pt.x, pt.y, st.brushR, st.pigment, st.strength);\n    return;\n  }\n  if (paint.type === 'single') {\n    paintAt(pt.x, pt.y, st.brushR, paint.pigment, st.strength);\n    return;\n  }\n  // type === 'mix' \u2014 three weights for rose/yellow/blue\n  const w = paint.weights;\n  // Pigment indices 0/1/2 match the order of weights[r, y, b]\n  if (w[0] > 0.005) paintAt(pt.x, pt.y, st.brushR, 0, st.strength * w[0]);\n  if (w[1] > 0.005) paintAt(pt.x, pt.y, st.brushR, 1, st.strength * w[1]);\n  if (w[2] > 0.005) paintAt(pt.x, pt.y, st.brushR, 2, st.strength * w[2]);\n}",

    "input": {
      "description": "Update last paragraph + insert approximation section",
      "new_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Backwards compatibility:</strong> the chroma-key\n          matching is strict, so existing SVGs that use black strokes,\n          neutral fills, or arbitrary colors don't trip the trigger\n          path &mdash; they fall through to <code>opts.pigment</code>\n          exactly as before. For a paranoid escape hatch (e.g. you\n          have an SVG that happens to use these pure primaries for\n          unrelated reasons), pass <code>triggerColors: false</code>.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Approximated color mixing (v0.43+)</h3>\n        <p>\n          Pass <code>approximateColor: true</code> to decompose any\n          parseable color into a mix of rose+yellow+blue and paint it\n          that way. Useful for SVGs authored with a full palette\n          (logos, illustrations, hand-drawn sketches with shading)\n          where you want the watercolor output to look approximately\n          like the original colors instead of forcing everything to\n          one pigment.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The algorithm is subtractive color theory: each pigment\n          absorbs its complementary wavelength, so the weight needed\n          for a given target color is one minus the channel that\n          pigment absorbs:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">rose weight   = 1 - g    // rose \u2248 magenta, absorbs green\nyellow weight = 1 - b    // yellow,         absorbs blue\nblue weight   = 1 - r    // blue \u2248 cyan,    absorbs red</pre>\n        <table class=\"docs-table\">\n          <thead><tr><th>Input color</th><th>RGB</th><th>Rose</th><th>Yellow</th><th>Blue</th><th>Result</th></tr></thead>\n          <tbody>\n            <tr><td>orange</td>  <td><code>#FF8000</code></td><td>0.50</td><td>1.00</td><td>0.00</td><td>orange (rose+yellow)</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>green</td>   <td><code>#008000</code></td><td>0.50</td><td>1.00</td><td>1.00</td><td>muted dark green</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>purple</td>  <td><code>#800080</code></td><td>1.00</td><td>0.50</td><td>0.50</td><td>purple (rose-dominant mix)</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>pink</td>    <td><code>#FFC0CB</code></td><td>0.25</td><td>0.20</td><td>0.00</td><td>pale rose-tinted</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>brown</td>   <td><code>#A52A2A</code></td><td>0.84</td><td>0.84</td><td>0.35</td><td>warm brown (heavy rose+yellow)</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>black</td>   <td><code>#000000</code></td><td>1.00</td><td>1.00</td><td>1.00</td><td>dark muddy (all three at max)</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>grey</td>    <td><code>#808080</code></td><td>0.50</td><td>0.50</td><td>0.50</td><td>brownish grey</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>white</td>   <td><code>#FFFFFF</code></td><td>0.00</td><td>0.00</td><td>0.00</td><td>no paint (paper shows)</td></tr>\n          </tbody>\n        </table>\n        <p>\n          <strong>How it deposits.</strong> Each approximated point\n          fires up to three <code>paintAt</code> calls &mdash; one per\n          non-zero weight &mdash; at strength scaled by that weight.\n          The simulation's Kubelka-Munk compositor then renders the\n          combined deposited pigment as the approximated color\n          naturally. No special \"blend\" logic; the physics handles it.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Wetness consequence.</strong> A mix-paint stamp can\n          fire 2&ndash;3 <code>paintAt</code> calls at the same point,\n          which is 2&ndash;3&times; the wetness of a single-pigment\n          stamp. Mid-saturation colors and darks bleed more. This is\n          actually accurate to real watercolor &mdash; mixing two\n          pigments physically does deposit more wet paint than using\n          one. If it's too much for a given use case, scale brush\n          <code>waterLoad</code> down or compose the mix in a drier\n          state via <code>paperWetness('damp')</code>.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Color parsing.</strong> The approximation path\n          accepts:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Hex 3-digit (<code>#F80</code>) and 6-digit (<code>#FF8000</code>)</li>\n          <li><code>rgb(r, g, b)</code> and <code>rgba(r, g, b, a)</code> (alpha ignored), absolute or percentage</li>\n          <li>Common CSS named colors: <code>orange</code>, <code>purple</code>, <code>pink</code>, <code>brown</code>, <code>gray</code>/<code>grey</code>, <code>black</code>, <code>white</code>, <code>silver</code>, <code>gold</code>, <code>coral</code>, <code>salmon</code>, <code>khaki</code>, <code>turquoise</code>, <code>crimson</code>, <code>navy</code>, <code>teal</code>, <code>olive</code>, <code>maroon</code>, <code>indigo</code>, <code>violet</code>, <code>skyblue</code>, <code>lightblue</code>, <code>lightgreen</code>, <code>darkgreen</code>, <code>darkblue</code>, <code>darkred</code>, <code>green</code>, <code>cyan</code>/<code>aqua</code>, plus the trigger names (<code>red</code>, <code>yellow</code>, <code>blue</code>, <code>lime</code>, <code>magenta</code>/<code>fuchsia</code>).</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Colors that don't parse (unknown names, malformed hex, HSL,\n          named gradients) fall through to <code>opts.pigment</code>\n          like they did pre-v0.43.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Triggers + approximation together.</strong> Both\n          flags can be on simultaneously. Resolution order per\n          element:\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>If <code>triggerColors</code> and stroke matches a trigger primary &rarr; use that pigment</li>\n          <li>If <code>approximateColor</code> and stroke parses to a color &rarr; decompose into mix</li>\n          <li>If <code>triggerColors</code> and fill matches a trigger primary &rarr; use that pigment</li>\n          <li>If <code>approximateColor</code> and fill parses to a color &rarr; decompose into mix</li>\n          <li>Walk up to parent <code>&lt;g&gt;</code> and repeat</li>\n          <li>Final fallback: <code>opts.pigment</code> or <code>currentPigment</code></li>\n        </ol>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Common pairing.</strong> Setting both\n          <code>triggerColors: true</code> and\n          <code>approximateColor: true</code> lets a designer use\n          chroma-key colors for \"I want this exact pigment\" (e.g.\n          <code>#FF00FF</code> for a rainbow accent) and any other\n          colors for naturalistic mixing. Best of both worlds.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Easing curves for drawing animation</h3>",
      "old_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Backwards compatibility:</strong> the chroma-key\n          matching is strict, so existing SVGs that use black strokes,\n          neutral fills, or arbitrary colors don't trip the new path\n          &mdash; they fall through to <code>opts.pigment</code>\n          exactly as before.     "input": {
      "description": "Add v0.43 changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.43 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-43\">v0.43 &mdash; Approximated color mixing for SVG</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>New <code>approximateColor</code> option (default false).</strong> When on, any SVG element with a parseable fill/stroke color that isn't an exact trigger primary gets decomposed into a mix of rose+yellow+blue and painted as such. Lets a multi-color SVG render with naturalistic watercolor mixing instead of forcing everything to one pigment.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>The algorithm:</strong> subtractive color theory. Each pigment absorbs its complementary wavelength, so the weight needed for a target color is one minus the channel that pigment absorbs:\n            <pre class=\"docs-code\">rose weight   = 1 - g    // rose \u2248 magenta, absorbs green\nyellow weight = 1 - b    // yellow,         absorbs blue\nblue weight   = 1 - r    // blue \u2248 cyan,    absorbs red</pre>\n            Worked examples: <code>#FF8000</code> orange \u2192 50% rose + 100% yellow; <code>#800080</code> purple \u2192 100% rose + 50% yellow + 50% blue; pure white \u2192 no paint; pure black \u2192 max of all three (dark muddy).\n          </li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Deposition via multi-stamp.</strong> Each approximated point fires up to three <code>paintAt</code> calls (one per non-zero weight), each at strength scaled by the weight. The simulation's Kubelka-Munk compositor renders the combined deposited pigment as the approximated color naturally &mdash; no special blend logic, the physics handles it.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Renamed <code>colorMap</code> &rarr; <code>triggerColors</code>.</strong> Better name for \"strict chroma-key matching of pure primaries to specific pigments.\" The old <code>colorMap</code> name still works as a deprecated alias for one release. Behavior is unchanged.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Both flags can be on simultaneously.</strong> Common pairing: <code>triggerColors: true, approximateColor: true</code> &mdash; chroma-key colors win for \"I want this exact pigment\" (rainbow accent at <code>#FF00FF</code>, etc.) and any other authored color gets naturalistic mixing. Best of both worlds.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Expanded color parser <code>_parseSvgColor</code>.</strong> Accepts 3-digit and 6-digit hex, <code>rgb()</code> and <code>rgba()</code> (alpha ignored, absolute and percentage forms), and ~35 common CSS named colors (orange, purple, pink, brown, gray/grey, gold, coral, salmon, khaki, turquoise, crimson, navy, teal, olive, maroon, indigo, violet, skyblue, lightblue, lightgreen, darkgreen, darkblue, darkred, plus black/white/silver and the trigger names). Unparseable colors fall through to <code>opts.pigment</code> like before.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Refactored <code>_colorToPigment</code>.</strong> Now uses the shared <code>_parseSvgColor</code> internally, then checks for exact trigger primary matches. Same strict-match semantics as v0.41/v0.42 &mdash; no behavior change for trigger paths.</li>\n          <li>Verified: 44 unit tests on parser + mix decomposition + trigger handling all passing.</li>\n          <li><strong>Wetness caveat:</strong> a mix-paint stamp can fire 2&ndash;3 paintAt calls at the same point, which is 2&ndash;3&times; the wetness of a single-pigment stamp. Mid-saturation colors and darks bleed more. This is accurate to real watercolor &mdash; mixing pigments physically deposits more wet paint &mdash; but if it's too much for a given use case, scale <code>waterLoad</code> down or use <code>paperWetness('damp')</code>.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.42 ===== -->",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.43 shipped.** Two SVG-loader options, both controllable independently.\n\n## The two options\n\n```js\nwc.traceSVG(svgText, {\n  triggerColors: true,        // (default) strict chroma-key match\n  approximateColor: false,    // (default) only triggers; arbitrary colors fall to opts.pigment\n});\n\nwc.traceSVG(svgText, {\n  triggerColors: false,       // ignore the trigger primaries entirely\n  approximateColor: true,     // decompose ALL colors into pigment mixes\n});\n\nwc.traceSVG(svgText, {\n  triggerColors: true,        // exact triggers win where they match\n  approximateColor: true,     // everything else gets mixed\n});\n```\n\nThe third combination \u2014 both on \u2014 is probably the most useful default for naturalistic SVGs that occasionally want a chroma-key accent. Designers can use `#FF00FF` for a rainbow flourish and any other authored color for naturalistic mixing.\n\n## The mix algorithm\n\nSubtractive color theory, three lines:\n\n```\nrose weight   = 1 - g    // rose \u2248 magenta, absorbs green\nyellow weight = 1 - b    // yellow,         absorbs blue\nblue weight   = 1 - r    // blue \u2248 cyan,    absorbs red\n```\n\nThe intuition: each pigment absorbs its complementary wavelength. To make a color appear, deposit pigment that absorbs everything *except* that color. White wants no pigment (no absorption needed). Red wants rose + yellow (absorbs green and blue, leaving red). Green wants yellow + blue. Black wants all three at max.\n\nSome worked examples (computed from the actual implementation):\n\n| Color | RGB | Rose | Yellow | Blue | Result |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `#FF8000` orange | (1, 0.5, 0) | 0.50 | 1.00 | 0.00 | rose + heavy yellow \u2192 orange |\n| `#008000` green | (0, 0.5, 0) | 0.50 | 1.00 | 1.00 | muted dark green |\n| `#800080` purple | (0.5, 0, 0.5) | 1.00 | 0.50 | 0.50 | rose-dominant purple |\n| `#FFC0CB` pink | (1, 0.75, 0.8) | 0.25 | 0.20 | 0.00 | pale rose-tinted |\n| `#000000` black | (0, 0, 0) | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 | dark muddy brown |\n| `#FFFFFF` white | (1, 1, 1) | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | no paint |\n\nThe interesting case is **black**: real watercolor \"black\" is achieved by mixing complementary colors heavily \u2014 that's what gives it character vs. an opaque pigment-black. Our decomposition handles this correctly: max rose + max yellow + max blue \u2192 dark muddy brown that reads as \"watercolor black.\"\n\n## How deposition works\n\nEach approximated point fires up to three `paintAt` calls, one per non-zero weight, each at `strength \u00d7 weight`. The KM compositor then renders the combined deposited pigment as the approximated color. No special blend logic \u2014 the physics handles mixing through the existing per-cell K-M reflectance math.\n\nThe trade-off is **wetness**: a mix point can fire 2-3\u00d7 the paintAt calls of a single-pigment point, which means 2-3\u00d7 the water + pressure at that location. Mid-saturation colors and darks bleed noticeably more than triggers.\n\nThis is actually accurate to real watercolor \u2014 mixing two pigments physically deposits more wet paint than using one. If it's too much for a specific use case, the escape valves are:\n- `waterLoad(0.5)` to halve the water-per-stamp before tracing\n- `paperWetness('damp')` to make the paper less absorbent\n- `paintLoad` tweaks if pigment density is off\n\n## Parser coverage\n\nThe approximation path accepts:\n- **Hex**: 3-digit (`#F80`) and 6-digit (`#FF8000`)\n- **rgb()**: `rgb(255, 128, 0)`, `rgb(100%, 50%, 0%)`, comma or space separators\n- **rgba()**: alpha ignored\n- **~35 named CSS colors**: orange, purple, pink, brown, gray/grey, gold, coral, salmon, khaki, turquoise, crimson, navy, teal, olive, maroon, indigo, violet, skyblue, lightblue, lightgreen, darkgreen, darkblue, darkred, plus the trigger names.\n\nAnything unparseable (unknown names, HSL, gradients, currentColor) falls through to `opts.pigment` exactly like pre-v0.43.\n\n## Compatibility\n\n- `colorMap` is now a deprecated alias for `triggerColors`. Existing v0.41/v0.42 code keeps working unchanged.\n- Default behavior is unchanged: `triggerColors: true, approximateColor: false` matches v0.42's `colorMap: true`. SVGs with black strokes / neutral fills still fall through to `opts.pigment` like before.\n- 44/44 unit tests passing across the parser (21 cases), trigger matching (16 cases), and mix decomposition (7 cases).\n\n## Try it\n\nThe most expressive single-flag setup:\n\n```js\nwc.traceSVG(svg, {\n  triggerColors: true,\n  approximateColor: true,\n  durationMs: 1500,\n  easing: 'penStroke',\n});\n```\n\nNow a hand-drawn SVG of an orchard scene with brown branches, green leaves, red apples, and a blue sky paints with naturalistic watercolor mixing \u2014 but if you put one `#FF00FF` swatch somewhere, it'll get the rainbow brush. Both behaviors available, both reasonable, both opt-in.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.44 shipped.** That was a real miss on my part \u2014 I shipped the API surface in v0.43 and forgot that an unsurfaced option might as well not exist for someone using the panel rather than the console. Sorry about that.\n\n## What's there now\n\nTwo new toggle buttons in their own controls row, right after \"SVG path pause\" and before the main toggle row:\n\n```\n[ SVG trigger colors  (on) ]   [ Approximate color mix  (off) ]\n```\n\nBoth default to the same state as the API defaults \u2014 `triggerColors: true`, `approximateColor: false` \u2014 so loading an SVG with no toggle changes gives the same result as v0.43's `traceSVG(text)`. They read at trace time, like duration / easing / pause, so you can flip them between Load SVG presses and the same file will paint differently.\n\nThe four meaningful combinations:\n\n| Trigger | Approx | What you get |\n|---|---|---|\n| ON | OFF | **(default)** Triggers (#FF0000, etc.) go to their pigments; everything else uses the active brush |\n| ON | ON | Triggers win for exact matches; all other colors decompose into rose+yellow+blue mixes |\n| OFF | ON | All colors decompose into mixes; trigger primaries treated like any other color |\n| OFF | OFF | Every color falls to the active brush (the v0.40 \"single pigment for everything\" behavior) |\n\n## Implementation note\n\nThe toggle buttons are simple visual state \u2014 they don't call any API on click. That's because `triggerColors` and `approximateColor` are *per-call options* on `traceSVG()`, not stateful settings on the `wc` instance. There's no `wc.triggerColors(true)` to call. So the wiring just mirrors `aria-pressed` on the buttons and reads it when constructing the trace opts:\n\n```js\nfunction currentSVGTraceOpts() {\n  const opts = { /* duration, easing, pause */ };\n  opts.triggerColors    = svgTriggerBtn.getAttribute('aria-pressed') === 'true';\n  opts.approximateColor = svgApproxBtn.getAttribute('aria-pressed') === 'true';\n  return opts;\n}\n```\n\nSame pattern as the easing dropdown and the pause slider. The lib gets the values at trace time.\n\n## Try the four corners\n\nThe fastest way to feel the difference: load the same multicolor SVG with each combination of toggles. With approximation on, the same source file becomes a different painting depending on whether trigger colors get their special treatment or get decomposed alongside everything else. With both off, you get the \"monochrome trace in the current brush color\" classic behavior.\n\nIf the toggles feel like they should remember state across reloads, a `localStorage` persistence wrapper would be 5 lines \u2014 let me know if that's wanted.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Replace single-paint resolver with stroke+fill resolver",
      "new_str": "// v0.43 \u2014 Decompose an RGB color into proportional weights of the\n// three watercolor pigments (rose, yellow, blue). Based on subtractive\n// color theory: each pigment absorbs its complementary wavelength.\n//   rose \u2248 magenta \u2192 absorbs green \u2192 weight = 1 - g\n//   yellow         \u2192 absorbs blue  \u2192 weight = 1 - b\n//   blue \u2248 cyan    \u2192 absorbs red   \u2192 weight = 1 - r\n// The result feeds the multi-stamp painter in svgTraceStep, where each\n// non-zero weight produces one paintAt call at strength * weight.\n// Brighter (closer to white) colors \u2192 smaller weights \u2192 less pigment.\n// Darker colors \u2192 larger weights \u2192 more pigment (and more wetness).\n// Pure white returns all zeros (no paint at all).\nfunction _rgbToPigmentMix(rgb) {\n  return [\n    Math.max(0, Math.min(1, 1 - rgb[1])),  // rose\n    Math.max(0, Math.min(1, 1 - rgb[2])),  // yellow\n    Math.max(0, Math.min(1, 1 - rgb[0])),  // blue\n  ];\n}\n\n// v0.45 \u2014 Convert a single CSS color string into a paint descriptor.\n// Returns one of:\n//   { type: 'single', pigment: idx }       \u2014 exact trigger primary\n//   { type: 'mix',    weights: [r, y, b] } \u2014 CMY decomposition\n//   null                                   \u2014 not parseable or unwanted\nfunction _resolveColorToPaint(colorStr, triggerColors, approximateColor) {\n  if (!colorStr || colorStr === 'none' || colorStr === 'transparent') {\n    return null;\n  }\n  if (triggerColors) {\n    const pig = _colorToPigment(colorStr);\n    if (pig !== null) return { type: 'single', pigment: pig };\n  }\n  if (approximateColor) {\n    const rgb = _parseSvgColor(colorStr);\n    if (rgb !== null) return { type: 'mix', weights: _rgbToPigmentMix(rgb) };\n  }\n  return null;\n}\n\n// v0.41 / v0.43 / v0.45 \u2014 Walk up the SVG DOM tree from a geometry\n// element collecting both the stroke and fill paint descriptors. Both\n// resolve independently \u2014 an element with stroke=\"red\" and fill=\"blue\"\n// returns paints for both. Either can be null if no recognized color is\n// found.\n//\n// Inheritance: walks up through parent <g> groups, taking the first\n// stroke/fill value found at any ancestor. Explicit \"none\" or\n// \"transparent\" on a closer ancestor stops the walk for that property\n// (no further inheritance), matching SVG cascade semantics.\nfunction _resolveSvgPaints(el, svgRoot, triggerColors, approximateColor) {\n  let strokePaint = null, fillPaint = null;\n  let strokeBlocked = false, fillBlocked = false;\n  let cur = el;\n  for (let depth = 0; cur && cur !== svgRoot && depth < 32; depth++) {\n    if (!strokePaint && !strokeBlocked) {\n      const v = _getSvgPresentationValue(cur, 'stroke');\n      if (v === 'none' || v === 'transparent') {\n        strokeBlocked = true;\n      } else if (v) {\n        strokePaint = _resolveColorToPaint(v, triggerColors, approximateColor);\n        // If it was a string but not recognized, keep walking \u2014 an\n        // ancestor might have a recognized value.\n      }\n    }\n    if (!fillPaint && !fillBlocked) {\n      const v = _getSvgPresentationValue(cur, 'fill');\n      if (v === 'none' || v === 'transparent') {\n        fillBlocked = true;\n      } else if (v) {\n        fillPaint = _resolveColorToPaint(v, triggerColors, approximateColor);\n      }\n    }\n    if ((strokePaint || strokeBlocked) && (fillPaint || fillBlocked)) break;\n    cur = cur.parentNode;\n  }\n  return { stroke: strokePaint, fill: fillPaint, strokeBlocked, fillBlocked };\n}",
      "old_str": "// v0.43 \u2014 Decompose an RGB color into proportional weights of the\n// three watercolor pigments (rose, yellow, blue). Based on subtractive\n// color theory: each pigment absorbs its complementary wavelength.\n//   rose \u2248 magenta \u2192 absorbs green \u2192 weight = 1 - g\n//   yellow         \u2192 absorbs blue  \u2192 weight = 1 - b\n//   blue \u2248 cyan    \u2192 absorbs red   \u2192 weight = 1 - r\n// The result feeds the multi-stamp painter in svgTraceStep, where each\n// non-zero weight produces one paintAt call at strength * weight.\n// Brighter (closer to white) colors \u2192 smaller weights \u2192 less pigment.\n// Darker colors \u2192 larger weights \u2192 more pigment (and more wetness).\n// Pure white returns all zeros (no paint at all).\nfunction _rgbToPigmentMix(rgb) {\n  return [\n    Math.max(0, Math.min(1, 1 - rgb[1])),  // rose\n    Math.max(0, Math.min(1, 1 - rgb[2])),  // yellow\n    Math.max(0, Math.min(1, 1 - rgb[0])),  // blue\n  ];\n}\n\n// v0.41 / v0.43 \u2014 Walk up the SVG DOM tree from the geometry element\n// looking for a paint specification in stroke (preferred \u2014 most SVG\n// \"sketches\" use line drawings) or fill. Returns one of:\n//\n//   { type: 'single', pigment: number }   \u2014 exact trigger color matched\n//   { type: 'mix',    weights: [r, y, b] } \u2014 approximate-color mix\n//   { type: 'single', pigment: fallback }  \u2014 no recognized color found\n//\n// `triggerColors` (default true) enables the strict chroma-key lookup\n// for the five pure primaries. `approximateColor` (default false)\n// enables the CMY-based mix decomposition for any parseable color.\n// Both can be on simultaneously \u2014 triggers win for exact matches, then\n// approximation fills in for anything else.\nfunction _resolveSvgPaint(el, svgRoot, fallback, triggerColors, approximateColor) {\n  let cur = el;\n  for (let depth = 0; cur && cur !== svgRoot && depth < 32; depth++) {\n    // STROKE first \u2014 line-drawing SVGs primarily encode color in stroke\n    const stroke = _getSvgPresentationValue(cur, 'stroke');\n    if (stroke && stroke !== 'none' && stroke !== 'transparent') {\n      if (triggerColors) {\n        const pig = _colorToPigment(stroke);\n        if (pig !== null) return { type: 'single', pigment: pig };\n      }\n      if (approximateColor) {\n        const rgb = _parseSvgColor(stroke);\n        if (rgb !== null) {\n          return { type: 'mix', weights: _rgbToPigmentMix(rgb) };\n        }\n      }\n    }\n    // FILL second \u2014 filled-shape SVGs (logos, regions)\n    const fill = _getSvgPresentationValue(cur, 'fill');\n    if (fill && fill !== 'none' && fill !== 'transparent') {\n      if (triggerColors) {\n        const pig = _colorToPigment(fill);\n        if (pig !== null) return { type: 'single', pigment: pig };\n      }\n      if (approximateColor) {\n        const rgb = _parseSvgColor(fill);\n        if (rgb !== null) {\n          return { type: 'mix', weights: _rgbToPigmentMix(rgb) };\n        }\n      }\n    }\n    cur = cur.parentNode;\n  }\n  return { type: 'single', pigment: fallback };\n}",

      "new_str": "// v0.45 \u2014 Convert a single CSS color string into a paint descriptor.\n// Returns one of:\n//   { type: 'single', pigment: idx }       \u2014 exact trigger primary\n//   { type: 'mix',    weights: [r, y, b] } \u2014 CMY decomposition\n//   null                                   \u2014 not parseable or unwanted\nfunction _resolveColorToPaint(colorStr, triggerColors, approximateColor) {\n  if (!colorStr || colorStr === 'none' || colorStr === 'transparent') {\n    return null;\n  }\n  if (triggerColors) {\n    const pig = _colorToPigment(colorStr);\n    if (pig !== null) return { type: 'single', pigment: pig };\n  }\n  if (approximateColor) {\n    const rgb = _parseSvgColor(colorStr);\n    if (rgb !== null) return { type: 'mix', weights: _rgbToPigmentMix(rgb) };\n  }\n  return null;\n}\n\n// v0.41 / v0.43 / v0.45 \u2014 Walk up the SVG DOM tree collecting both\n// stroke and fill paint descriptors. Returns { stroke, fill,\n// strokeBlocked, fillBlocked } where each paint is either a descriptor\n// or null. Inheritance: walks up through parent <g> groups, taking the\n// first recognized value found at any ancestor. An explicit \"none\" or\n// \"transparent\" closer to the element blocks further inheritance for\n// that property \u2014 matches SVG cascade semantics.\nfunction _resolveSvgPaints(el, svgRoot, triggerColors, approximateColor) {\n  let strokePaint = null, fillPaint = null;\n  let strokeBlocked = false, fillBlocked = false;\n  let cur = el;\n  for (let depth = 0; cur && cur !== svgRoot && depth < 32; depth++) {\n    if (!strokePaint && !strokeBlocked) {\n      const v = _getSvgPresentationValue(cur, 'stroke');\n      if (v === 'none' || v === 'transparent') {\n        strokeBlocked = true;\n      } else if (v) {\n        strokePaint = _resolveColorToPaint(v, triggerColors, approximateColor);\n        // If string is present but unrecognized, keep walking \u2014 an\n        // ancestor might have a recognized stroke.\n      }\n    }\n    if (!fillPaint && !fillBlocked) {\n      const v = _getSvgPresentationValue(cur, 'fill');\n      if (v === 'none' || v === 'transparent') {\n        fillBlocked = true;\n      } else if (v) {\n        fillPaint = _resolveColorToPaint(v, triggerColors, approximateColor);\n      }\n    }\n    if ((strokePaint || strokeBlocked) && (fillPaint || fillBlocked)) break;\n    cur = cur.parentNode;\n  }\n  return { stroke: strokePaint, fill: fillPaint, strokeBlocked, fillBlocked };\n}",

      "new_str": "  const triggerColors = opts.triggerColors !== undefined\n    ? !!opts.triggerColors\n    : (opts.colorMap !== undefined ? !!opts.colorMap : true);\n  const approximateColor = opts.approximateColor === true;\n\n  // v0.45 \u2014 Hoist scale + offsets BEFORE the loop. Previously these\n  // were computed AFTER, but the fill-stamp path needs them during\n  // element iteration to convert SVG-coordinate sample spacing into\n  // a grid-radius-relative density.\n  const sx = (GW * SVG_TRACE_FIT) / vbW;\n  const sy = (GH * SVG_TRACE_FIT) / vbH;\n  const sFit = Math.min(sx, sy);\n  const offsetX_pre = GW * 0.5 - (vbW * sFit) * 0.5;\n  const offsetY_pre = GH * 0.5 - (vbH * sFit) * 0.5;\n\n  // v0.45 \u2014 Pre-resolve brush radius (in grid cells) so fill sampling\n  // can space its stamps relative to it. Outline-tracing uses the same\n  // radius downstream.\n  const brushPx = opts.brushSize !== undefined ? +opts.brushSize : brushSize;\n  const brushR_pre = (brushPx / 2) / SCALE;\n\n  // v0.45 \u2014 Fill-shapes mode. When ON (default), elements with a\n  // non-trivial fill get their INTERIOR stamped \u2014 sample points in a\n  // regular grid over the bounding box, keep the ones inside the path,\n  // and paintAt each with the fill paint. Combines naturally with the\n  // outline-tracing path: a shape with both stroke and fill traces its\n  // outline AND stamps its interior. Pure-stroke shapes only outline.\n  // Pure-fill shapes only stamp.\n  const fillShapes = opts.fillShapes !== false;\n  // Fill-stamp spacing in SVG units, derived from brush radius in grid\n  // units. The factor (0.85) controls overlap: 1.0 = stamps just touch,\n  // <1.0 = stamps overlap (denser coverage), >1.0 = gaps between stamps\n  // (dappled). 0.85 is a tight but not redundant default.\n  const fillSpacingSvg = sFit > 0 ? (brushR_pre * 0.85) / sFit : 0;\n\n  const geomEls = svg.querySelectorAll('path,line,polyline,polygon,rect,circle,ellipse');\n  for (const el of geomEls) {\n    const d = pathStringFromSvgElement(el);\n    if (!d) continue;\n    const p = document.createElementNS(svgNS, 'path');\n    p.setAttribute('d', d);\n    tmpSvg.appendChild(p);\n    let len = 0;\n    try { len = p.getTotalLength(); } catch (e) { tmpSvg.removeChild(p); continue; }\n    if (!isFinite(len) || len <= 0) { tmpSvg.removeChild(p); continue; }\n\n    // v0.45 \u2014 Resolve stroke and fill paints independently.\n    let strokePaint = null, fillPaint = null;\n    if (triggerColors || approximateColor) {\n      const paints = _resolveSvgPaints(el, svg, triggerColors, approximateColor);\n      strokePaint = paints.stroke;\n      fillPaint   = paints.fill;\n    }\n    // For backwards compatibility with the v0.41..v0.44 single-paint\n    // semantics: if neither resolved AND neither color flag is off,\n    // use the fallback as the OUTLINE paint (preserves \"trace this SVG\n    // in the active brush color\" for unstyled SVGs).\n    if (!strokePaint && !fillPaint) {\n      strokePaint = { type: 'single', pigment: fallbackPigment };\n    }\n\n    // v0.45 \u2014 FILL STAMPING. For elements with a recognized fill, sample\n    // a regular grid over the path's bounding box, keep points inside\n    // the fill, and add them to the points list with the fill paint.\n    // Fill stamps come BEFORE outline strokes in the array, so when\n    // both are present, the outline appears on top of the fill (which\n    // is the natural watercolor \"wash then ink the edge\" order).\n    if (fillShapes && fillPaint && fillSpacingSvg > 0) {\n      let bbox = null;\n      try { bbox = p.getBBox(); } catch (e) { bbox = null; }\n      if (bbox && bbox.width > 0 && bbox.height > 0) {\n        // Hard cap on samples per shape \u2014 protects against pathological\n        // SVGs with huge viewBoxes and tiny brush radius that would\n        // try to stamp millions of points. 10000 stamps is plenty for\n        // any reasonable fill.\n        const xCount = Math.ceil(bbox.width / fillSpacingSvg);\n        const yCount = Math.ceil(bbox.height / fillSpacingSvg);\n        const tooMany = xCount * yCount > 10000;\n        if (!tooMany) {\n          for (let yy = bbox.y; yy <= bbox.y + bbox.height; yy += fillSpacingSvg) {\n            for (let xx = bbox.x; xx <= bbox.x + bbox.width; xx += fillSpacingSvg) {\n              let inside = false;\n              try {\n                // Modern API: pass a DOMPointInit { x, y }\n                inside = p.isPointInFill({ x: xx, y: yy });\n              } catch (e) {\n                // Older browsers want an SVGPoint\n                try {\n                  const root = tmpSvg.ownerSVGElement || tmpSvg;\n                  if (root.createSVGPoint) {\n                    const pt = root.createSVGPoint();\n                    pt.x = xx; pt.y = yy;\n                    inside = p.isPointInFill(pt);\n                  }\n                } catch (e2) { /* give up, skip this point */ }\n              }\n              if (inside) {\n                allPoints.push({ x: xx, y: yy });\n                allPaints.push(fillPaint);\n              }\n            }\n          }\n          // Mark end of the fill segment as a path boundary so per-\n          // stroke pauses work correctly across the fill/outline split.\n          if (allPoints.length > (pathStartsRaw[pathStartsRaw.length - 1] || 0)) {\n            pathStartsRaw.push(allPoints.length);\n          }\n        }\n      }\n    }\n\n    // OUTLINE TRACING (the original behavior). Skip if there's no\n    // stroke paint AND a fill was already stamped \u2014 outlining an\n    // already-stamped shape in some non-recognized stroke color would\n    // be confusing.     "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Insert filled-shape stamping section",
      "new_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Common pairing.</strong> Setting both\n          <code>triggerColors: true</code> and\n          <code>approximateColor: true</code> lets a designer use\n          chroma-key colors for \"I want this exact pigment\" (e.g.\n          <code>#FF00FF</code> for a rainbow accent) and any other\n          colors for naturalistic mixing. Best of both worlds.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Filled shape stamping (v0.45+)</h3>\n        <p>\n          Before v0.45 the SVG loader only traced <strong>outlines</strong>\n          &mdash; it sampled points along each element's perimeter and\n          painted them in order. Stroked SVGs (line drawings, contours,\n          calligraphic strokes) worked beautifully. Filled SVGs (logos,\n          icons, regions with solid colors and no stroke) were\n          effectively invisible: the loader traced the outline using\n          the fill color, but a one-pixel-thick outline doesn't read as\n          \"a filled red square.\"\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          v0.45 changes that. <code>fillShapes: true</code> (default)\n          adds a <strong>fill pass</strong> alongside the outline pass.\n          For each element with a recognized fill paint, the loader:\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Computes the element's bounding box via <code>p.getBBox()</code></li>\n          <li>Samples a regular grid of points across the bounding box, spaced at <code>0.85 &times; brushRadius</code> in grid units (tight, slightly overlapping coverage)</li>\n          <li>For each sample, calls <code>p.isPointInFill({x, y})</code> &mdash; the browser's exact fill-test, including holes via the fill-rule</li>\n          <li>Keeps the inside points and adds them to the trace list with the fill paint</li>\n        </ol>\n        <p>\n          The fill samples come <strong>before</strong> outline samples\n          in the array, so when both are present the outline appears on\n          top of the fill &mdash; the natural watercolor \"wash first,\n          ink the edge after\" order. Filled+stroked elements get both.\n          Pure-fill elements just get stamped. Pure-stroke elements\n          just get outlined (unchanged from v0.44).\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Stroke and fill resolve independently.</strong> Before\n          v0.45 the resolver collapsed both to a single paint: it walked\n          looking for any recognized stroke OR fill and used the first\n          hit. v0.45 splits them. An element with <code>stroke=\"blue\"\n          fill=\"yellow\"</code> resolves to {stroke: blue, fill: yellow}\n          &mdash; the interior stamps in yellow and the outline traces\n          in blue. <code>fill=\"none\"</code> explicitly blocks fill\n          inheritance up the tree; <code>stroke=\"none\"</code> blocks\n          stroke inheritance.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Sample density &amp; cap.</strong> Spacing of\n          <code>0.85 &times; brushRadius</code> means roughly 1.4 stamps\n          per brush diameter. Dense enough for solid coverage with\n          natural watercolor variation, sparse enough to avoid\n          oversampling. There's a 10000-sample hard cap per element to\n          protect against pathological SVGs (huge viewBoxes + tiny\n          brush). Hitting the cap silently skips the fill for that\n          element and continues with the outline.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Wetness consequence.</strong> A large filled shape\n          fires hundreds to thousands of <code>paintAt</code> calls in\n          its bounding box. Combined with <code>approximateColor</code>,\n          each can become 2&ndash;3 calls per stamp (one per pigment\n          weight). The simulation handles this fine, but if you're\n          stamping a wide solid wash of a dark color, expect a lot of\n          wet pigment migration during the trace. <code>paperWetness('dry')</code>\n          before the trace gives crisper edges; <code>paperWetness('wet')</code>\n          beforehand encourages dramatic bleeding.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Off-switch.</strong> <code>fillShapes: false</code>\n          falls back to the v0.44 behavior: if an element has a fill\n          but no stroke, the outline gets traced in the fill color\n          (which is invisible for typical filled SVGs &mdash; use this\n          flag only if you specifically want the old behavior). Filled+\n          stroked elements still trace just the outline in this mode.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Easing curves for drawing animation</h3>",
      "old_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Common pairing.</strong> Setting both\n          <code>triggerColors: true</code> and\n          <code>approximateColor: true</code> lets a designer use\n          chroma-key colors for \"I want this exact pigment\" (e.g.\n          <code>#FF00FF</code> for a rainbow accent) and any other\n          colors for naturalistic mixing. Best of both worlds.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Easing curves for drawing animation</h3>",

    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Add v0.45 changelog entry",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.45 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-45\">v0.45 &mdash; SVG filled shape stamping</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Filled SVGs now actually paint.</strong> Before v0.45, the loader only traced perimeters &mdash; great for line drawings, useless for icons / logos / region maps. Filled-only SVGs traced an invisible outline in the fill color and the canvas looked empty. v0.45 adds a fill pass: for each element with a recognized fill paint, sample a grid of points across its bounding box, keep the ones inside the path via <code>p.isPointInFill({x,y})</code>, and stamp each with the fill paint. Sample spacing is 0.85&times; brush radius &mdash; tight, slightly overlapping coverage.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Stroke and fill resolve independently.</strong> The v0.41&ndash;v0.44 resolver collapsed both into a single paint (first hit wins, stroke preferred). v0.45 splits them. An element with <code>stroke=\"blue\" fill=\"yellow\"</code> resolves to {stroke: blue, fill: yellow} &mdash; the interior stamps in yellow and the outline traces in blue. Both happen for filled+stroked elements; pure-fill stamps; pure-stroke outlines.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Per-element order:</strong> fill stamps come BEFORE outline strokes in the trace queue, so the outline lands on top of the fill &mdash; the natural watercolor \"wash, then ink the edge\" sequence.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>New <code>fillShapes</code> option (default true).</strong> The new behavior is opt-out, not opt-in &mdash; most users want filled SVGs to work. <code>fillShapes: false</code> falls back to v0.44 behavior (filled-only shapes have their outline traced in the fill color).</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>UI toggle.</strong> A \"Fill shapes\" button joins the SVG controls row alongside \"Trigger colors\" and \"Approximate color mix.\" On by default.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>SVG cascade semantics for <code>none</code>:</strong> an explicit <code>fill=\"none\"</code> or <code>stroke=\"none\"</code> at any ancestor blocks further inheritance for that property, matching how browsers render SVG.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-perf\">perf</span> 10000-sample hard cap per element protects against pathological SVGs (huge viewBoxes + tiny brush radius). Hitting the cap silently skips the fill for that element and continues with the outline trace.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> Internal refactor: <code>_resolveSvgPaint</code> (single) replaced with <code>_resolveSvgPaints</code> (stroke + fill). New <code>_resolveColorToPaint(color, triggerColors, approximateColor)</code> helper shared by both. Cleaner code paths, identical behavior for the four trigger/approx combinations.</li>\n          <li><strong>Behavior change worth noting:</strong> in v0.41&ndash;v0.44, a filled-only SVG (no explicit stroke) traced its outline in the fill color. In v0.45 with default <code>fillShapes: true</code>, that same SVG stamps its interior instead. The previous outline-only behavior is recoverable via <code>fillShapes: false</code>.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.44 ===== -->",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.45 shipped.** Filled SVGs now paint as fills, not as invisible outlines. This was the real fix for the issue you were chasing through the toggle-confusion before.\n\n## What changed mechanically\n\nThe geometry loop used to do one thing per element: trace its outline using whichever color came first (stroke or fill). That worked for line-drawn SVGs because the strokes are visible. For filled SVGs (logos, icons, region maps), the outline path is conceptually present but visually nothing \u2014 a one-pixel-thick contour of a red rectangle reads as \"empty canvas,\" not \"red rectangle.\"\n\nv0.45 splits stroke and fill resolution into separate paints, then runs two passes per element:\n\n```\nfor each element:\n  resolve {stroke, fill} independently   \u2190 v0.45 split\n  if fillShapes && fill exists:\n    sample bbox at 0.85 \u00d7 brushRadius spacing\n    for each sample, p.isPointInFill({x,y}) \u2192 keep if inside\n    push samples with the fill paint\n  if stroke exists:\n    sample along path perimeter\n    push samples with the stroke paint\n```\n\nThe fill samples come **before** outline samples in the queue, so the outline lands on top of the fill \u2014 natural watercolor \"wash, then ink the edge\" sequence.\n\n## The four element kinds\n\n| Element has | Old behavior (v0.44) | New behavior (v0.45) |\n|---|---|---|\n| stroke only | outline traced in stroke color | outline traced in stroke color (unchanged) |\n| fill only | outline traced in **fill** color (often invisible) | **interior stamped in fill color** |\n| stroke + fill | outline traced in stroke (fill ignored) | interior stamped in fill **and** outline traced in stroke |\n| neither | outline traced in current brush | outline traced in current brush (unchanged) |\n\n## Implementation notes\n\n- `p.isPointInFill({x, y})` is the modern SVGGeometryElement method that does the exact fill-test using the path's `fill-rule` (handles holes, even-odd vs nonzero, all that complexity for free). Fallback to SVGPoint for older browsers.\n- Sample spacing of `0.85 \u00d7 brushRadius` gives roughly 1.4 stamps per brush diameter \u2014 solid coverage with natural watercolor variation, not so dense that it oversamples.\n- A 10000-sample hard cap per element protects against pathological combinations (huge viewBox + tiny brush). Hitting the cap silently skips the fill for that element.\n- Scale + offsets + brush radius are now computed **before** the element loop instead of after, because the fill-stamp path needs them during iteration.\n- SVG cascade for `none`: explicit `fill=\"none\"` or `stroke=\"none\"` at any ancestor blocks further inheritance for that property \u2014 matches browser rendering.\n\n## Test results\n\nEnd-to-end with 7 SVG variants, all producing correct paintAt distributions:\n\n| Input | fillShapes | Result |\n|---|---|---|\n| `<rect stroke=\"#FF0000\" fill=\"none\"/>` | true | 101 rose outline only \u2713 |\n| `<rect fill=\"#FF0000\"/>` | true | 49 rose interior stamps, no outline \u2713 |\n| `<rect fill=\"#FF0000\"/>` | false | 101 rose outline (v0.44 compat) \u2713 |\n| `<rect fill=\"#FF8000\"/>` + approximateColor | true | 49 rose + 49 yellow interior stamps \u2713 |\n| `<rect fill=\"#FFFF00\" stroke=\"#0000FF\"/>` | true | 49 yellow interior + 101 blue outline \u2713 |\n| `<rect/>` (unstyled) | true | 101 current-brush outline \u2713 |\n| `<rect fill=\"none\" stroke=\"red\"/>` | true | 101 rose outline \u2713 |\n\n## UI\n\nA third toggle joins the SVG controls row: **Fill shapes** (on by default). Off recovers v0.44 behavior for any user who specifically wants the old outline-in-fill-color trace.\n\n## Behavior change worth flagging\n\nFilled-only SVGs paint differently than they did in v0.44. The old behavior traced their outline in the fill color (which looked like nothing for typical filled icons). The new behavior stamps the interior. If you had a filled-only SVG that was producing some specific (likely invisible) result you wanted to preserve, pass `fillShapes: false` to get it back. For everyone else, this is the change you wanted.\n\nTry loading any filled icon, logo, or region map now and it should paint as watercolor fills with proper bleeding and color mixing through the simulation.",
    "citations": []
  }

    "text": "Already supported \u2014 the parser handles 3-digit hex on every code path:\n\n| Input | Result |\n|---|---|\n| `fill=\"#F00\"` | 49 rose interior stamps |\n| `fill=\"#0F0\"` | 49 mask stamps (lift sentinel) |\n| `fill=\"#F0F\"` | 49 rainbow stamps |\n| `stroke=\"#FF0\"` | 101 yellow outline points |\n| `fill=\"#F80\"` + approximateColor | 49 rose + 49 yellow stamps (orange mix) |\n| `style=\"fill:#f0f\"` | 49 rainbow stamps (lowercase + style attr) |\n| `fill=\"#fff\"` + approximateColor | 0 paints (white = no pigment) |\n\nThis has worked since v0.43 when I refactored the parser to share logic between trigger-matching and approximate-color decomposition. Both 3-digit (`#F0F`) and 6-digit (`#FF00FF`) get parsed by the same `_parseSvgColor` function, which expands 3-digit into 6-digit by doubling each nibble:\n\n```js\nm = s.match(/^#([0-9a-f])([0-9a-f])([0-9a-f])$/);\nif (m) return [\n  parseInt(m[1] + m[1], 16) / 255,   // F \u2192 FF \u2192 255\n  parseInt(m[2] + m[2], 16) / 255,\n  parseInt(m[3] + m[3], 16) / 255,\n];\n```\n\nSame rules: case-insensitive, works in `fill=` / `stroke=` attributes and in `style=\"fill:#...\"` strings, and gets recognized by both the trigger path (exact match against pure primaries) and the approximation path (CMY decomposition).\n\nIf you tried `#F0F` in your SVG and didn't see the rainbow brush, the issue is probably one of:\n- The element was filled but you were on v0.44 or earlier (the fill outline was traced invisibly \u2014 fixed in v0.45 with `fillShapes`)\n- The \"SVG trigger colors\" toggle was off in the UI\n- The color was inherited from a parent that had an explicit `fill=\"none\"` blocking it\n\nIf you'd like to share an example SVG that's misbehaving, I can run it through and tell you exactly which code path it's hitting.",
    "citations": []
  }
