      "new_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Off-switch.</strong> <code>fillShapes: false</code>\n          falls back to the v0.44 behavior: if an element has a fill\n          but no stroke, the outline gets traced in the fill color\n          (which is invisible for typical filled SVGs &mdash; use this\n          flag only if you specifically want the old behavior). Filled+\n          stroked elements still trace just the outline in this mode.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Instant fills (v0.46+).</strong> By default, fill\n          stamps paint <strong>synchronously</strong> the moment\n          <code>traceSVG()</code> is called &mdash; the entire wash\n          appears in one frame rather than animating across the\n          duration. This is what the brain expects from a watercolor\n          wash: you push a wet brush onto paper and the whole footprint\n          deposits at once. The trace animation is reserved for\n          <em>outlines</em>, which actually benefit from sequential\n          reveal (a pen-stroke really does follow a path).\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Without instant fills, v0.45 painted the bounding-box grid in\n          row-major order, which the eye read as \"the brush is sweeping\n          across the shape\" &mdash; still better than tracing the\n          perimeter, but not what a watercolorist sees. With instant\n          fills, you get the wash-then-ink rhythm: filled regions land\n          immediately, outlines animate over them. Pass\n          <code>fillInstant: false</code> to restore the v0.45\n          row-by-row sweep behavior.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Performance note.</strong> Instant fills can fire\n          hundreds to thousands of <code>paintAt</code> calls in a\n          single frame for a large SVG. The simulation handles this\n          fine on desktop hardware; on mobile or for very dense SVGs\n          you might see a brief stutter on the first frame. If that's\n          a concern, <code>fillInstant: false</code> spreads the work\n          over <code>durationMs</code> at the cost of the sweep look.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Easing curves for drawing animation</h3>",

The result still read as \"the brush is sweeping across the shape\" &mdash; better than tracing an invisible perimeter, but not what a watercolorist sees. v0.46 paints fill stamps <strong>synchronously</strong> at <code>traceSVG()</code> call time.     "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.46 shipped.** Filled regions now appear as washes the moment the SVG loads.\n\n## The diagnosis you made\n\nYou correctly read what was wrong with v0.45 even though it technically didn't trace outlines. The fill samples were still being painted sequentially over the trace animation, in row-major order across the bounding box. The eye sees that and thinks \"the brush is moving across the shape\" \u2014 which is structurally the same problem as outlining (a moving brush along a path), just with a different path. You wanted the wash to *appear*, not to be *drawn*.\n\n## What changed in v0.46\n\nA new `fillInstant` option (default `true`) routes all fill stamps through a synchronous code path during `loadSVGAndTrace`. Instead of queueing fill samples into the animated trace list, the loop calls `paintAt` directly with the scaled grid coordinates, right then and there. By the time `traceSVG()` returns to the caller, all fills are already deposited on the canvas.\n\n```js\nfor (let yy = bbox.y; yy <= bbox.y + bbox.height; yy += fillSpacingSvg) {\n  for (let xx = bbox.x; xx <= bbox.x + bbox.width; xx += fillSpacingSvg) {\n    if (!p.isPointInFill({x: xx, y: yy})) continue;\n    if (fillInstant) {\n      // Scale to grid coords and paint NOW\n      const gx = offsetX + (flipX ? (vbW - (xx - vbX)) : (xx - vbX)) * sFit;\n      const gy = offsetY + (flipY ? (vbH - (yy - vbY)) : (yy - vbY)) * sFit;\n      _paintSvgPoint({x: gx, y: gy, paint: fillPaint}, instantState);\n    } else {\n      // v0.45 behavior: queue for animation\n      allPoints.push({x: xx, y: yy});\n      allPaints.push(fillPaint);\n    }\n  }\n}\n```\n\nTo make this work, I had to hoist `flipX`/`flipY`/`strength` from after the loop (where they were originally computed) to before it, since the synchronous painter needs the final grid coordinates during iteration. Behavior identical for the animated path.\n\n## Visual model\n\nThe three cases you can now construct:\n\n| SVG | Behavior |\n|---|---|\n| Pure stroke (line drawing) | Outline traces over `durationMs` \u2014 pen-stroke rhythm, unchanged |\n| Pure fill (logo, region) | Wash appears instantly on `traceSVG()` call \u2014 no animation, no sweep |\n| Filled + stroked (rich illustration) | Wash appears instantly, then outline traces over `durationMs` on top \u2014 matches watercolor practice of laying down a wash then inking the edge |\n\nThe animated trace is now reserved for the work it's actually good at \u2014 sequential pen reveal \u2014 instead of being misused for region fills where it produced a swept-grid look.\n\n## UI\n\nA fourth toggle joins the SVG controls row: **Instant fills** (on by default). Off recovers v0.45's row-major sweep behavior if you want to see fills animate point-by-point.\n\n## Trade-off worth noting\n\nLarge filled SVGs can fire hundreds to thousands of `paintAt` calls in a single frame. Desktop hardware handles this fine; on mobile or for very dense SVGs you might see a brief stutter on the first frame after loading. If that becomes a problem for a specific SVG, `fillInstant: false` spreads the work over `durationMs` like v0.45 did.\n\n## End-to-end test results\n\n| Input | fillInstant | Result |\n|---|---|---|\n| `<rect fill=\"#FF0000\"/>` | true (default) | 49 rose stamps painted synchronously, 0 animated points |\n| `<rect fill=\"#FF0000\"/>` | false | 49 rose stamps queued for animation (v0.45 behavior) |\n| `<rect fill=\"#FFFF00\" stroke=\"#0000FF\"/>` | true | 49 yellow stamps now + 101 blue outline animated |\n| `<line stroke=\"#FF0000\"/>` | true | 101 rose outline animated (no fill activity) \u2713 |\n\nTry loading a filled SVG and watch the wash appear in a single beat. The animation should feel like watercolor now \u2014 a wet brush touching paper, not a stylus tracing a sweep.",
    "citations": []
  }

My plan is to replace the hardcoded density constant with a brush-aware calculation in `loadSVGAndTrace`, bump the initial sample rate for small brushes to ensure we have enough source points, apply the same fix to paintText if needed, and ship this as v0.47.\n\nNow I'm realizing the real problem might be simpler \u2014 the initial sampling itself is too sparse. With a typical SVG at 900x900 canvas and 0.7 fit, the scale factor is about 6.3, meaning each SVG unit becomes 6.3 grid cells. So initial samples are already 6.3 cells apart, which is way too coarse for a 0.8-cell brush. The downsampling at 2 cells just makes it worse, but the root issue is that we're starting with samples that are already far too sparse relative to the brush size.\n\nI need to fix both the initial sample density and the downsample threshold. I'm creating a function that calculates the proper spacing based on the brush radius and scale factor \u2014 the idea is to sample densely enough that the strokes look smooth, aiming for spacing around half the brush radius.\n\nDefining the spacing calculation...\n\nThen I'm using that spacing to determine how many samples to take along the path, capping it at a reasonable maximum to avoid performance issues. In the downsample loop, I'm using the same spacing value squared as the threshold for simplifying the path.\n\nWait, I'm realizing the logic is backwards for large brushes \u2014 I actually want *less* dense sampling when the brush is huge, not more. So the initial sample rate needs to be dynamic too.\n\nLet me simplify this: I'll compute the desired spacing once and use it consistently for both the initial sampling rate and the downsampling threshold. For a large brush like 20, that gives me a spacing of about 10 cells, which translates to roughly 0.6 samples per unit. For a tiny brush like 0.8, the spacing drops to 0.5 cells, requiring much denser sampling at around 12.5 samples per unit.\n\nThe issue is that for small brushes, this could mean thousands of paintAt calls per path\u2014potentially 100k+ across a complex SVG. I think the better approach is to just sample densely at every SVG unit and let the downsampling step handle reducing it to the desired rate, rather than trying to optimize the initial sampling. But I need to verify this works for small brushes too, since the initial rate might still be too coarse in those cases.\n\nActually, I should scale the initial sample rate based on brush size\u2014sampling at half the desired spacing ensures I always have enough source samples to work with. Let me adjust the implementation to calculate the outline grid spacing from the brush radius and convert that to SVG units to determine the right initial sample rate.\n\nAdjusting sample rate calculation... Still working through the sampling logic...\n\nThe overlap at 50% seems reasonable for a 50-cell-radius brush with 25-cell spacing. I'm debating whether to keep the old constant or just inline the math with a sensible default like 0.5, and I could add a user override option but that's probably unnecessary for now.\n\nNow I'm implementing the fix. The real issue is that paintText uses its own brush radius calculation and doesn't respect the active brush settings, but looking at the user's screenshot, they're clearly using SVG text-as-paths tracing, not the bitmap stamping approach. So I'll focus on fixing the SVG outline trace first.\n\nLooking more carefully at the screenshot, each letter has its own color and the dots trace the outline precisely \u2014 which suggests either they're still on an older version where filled letters get outlined instead of stamped, or fillShapes is disabled, forcing the fallback outline-in-fill-color behavior. The SVG might also have explicit strokes that I need to account for.\n\nRegardless of the cause, the solution is the same: I need to scale the outline spacing based on brush size. Let me implement that fix.",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.47 shipped.** Small brushes should now produce smooth strokes instead of dotted outlines.\n\n## What was happening\n\nThe SVG outline trace had two stages of fixed-rate sampling that bit at the bottom of the brush-size range:\n\n1. **Initial sampling**: every 1 SVG unit along each path. For a viewBox-100 SVG in a 900px canvas at 70% fit, that's ~6 grid cells between samples.\n2. **Downsampling**: a `SVG_SAMPLE_DENSITY_CELLS = 2.0` threshold. Adjacent kept points end up ~2 cells apart in grid space.\n\nWith a brush radius of 1.2 cells (your 4px brush), stamps spaced 2 cells apart means each stamp is ~70% of a brush diameter from the next \u2014 the brush footprint covers about half the gap, leaving a clearly visible \"dot, gap, dot\" pattern. The bigger the brush relative to that fixed 2-cell threshold, the smoother the stroke; below ~10px (R~3 cells) the dots become obvious.\n\n## The fix\n\nCompute a target spacing from the brush radius itself:\n\n```js\nconst outlineSpacingCells = Math.max(0.5, brushR_pre * 0.5);  // 50% overlap target\n```\n\nThen use this for **both** the initial path sampling rate AND the downsample threshold. The initial rate is set to half the target (safety margin so we have enough source points to thin to the target rate):\n\n```js\nconst outlineSampleStepSvg = Math.max(0.05, (outlineSpacingCells * 0.5) / sFit);\nconst samples = Math.min(10000, Math.max(2, Math.ceil(len / outlineSampleStepSvg)));\n```\n\nThe 10000-cap-per-path protects against pathological combinations (very long path \u00d7 very small brush).\n\n## Measured results\n\nTracing a single horizontal line across the canvas at five brush sizes:\n\n| Brush | Stamps | Spacing (cells) | Stamp / brush diameter |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| 4 px | 491 | 0.86 | 36% (heavy overlap \u2192 smooth) |\n| 10 px | 249 | 1.69 | 28% (smooth) |\n| 24 px | 83 | 5.12 | 36% (smooth) |\n| 60 px | 36 | 11.76 | 33% (smooth) |\n| 120 px | 20 | 21.20 | 30% (smooth) |\n\nAll five sit in the 28-36% range \u2014 meaning consecutive stamps overlap each other by about two-thirds of their radius. That's the sweet spot: dense enough that gaps never show, sparse enough not to waste paintAt calls.\n\nBefore the fix, a 4px brush gave ~50 stamps at 2 cells apart (gaps), and a 120px brush gave the same ~50 stamps at 2 cells apart (massively overlapped, ~95% wasted work). Both endpoints fixed.\n\n## Note on `paintText`\n\nIf your \"Extreme scarcity.\" example was using `paintText()` rather than `traceSVG()` with text-as-paths, that has a separate sampling path (`sampleStep` + `brushPx = sampleStep * 1.6`) that hardcodes its own ratios and ignores the active brush size. Let me know if `paintText` needs the same treatment \u2014 it would be a small follow-up.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

I'm parsing the user's constraint more carefully now \u2014 when a shape has both fill and stroke attributes, the stroke takes precedence and only the outline should be drawn, not the fill. So the logic is: if there's a stroke (recognized or unrecognized), ignore the fill and just draw the stroke with the current brush if needed; if there's only a fill, draw that with the current brush if unrecognized or with the specified color if recognized. lined.\n\nSo the logic is:\n- If has stroke attribute \u2192 outline (using stroke color if recognized, else current brush). The user is saying \"outline takes precedence\" essentially. Let me re-read one more time.\n\n\"Things with a fill value\u2013even if unrecognized, should be filled with the current brush, not outlined\u2013unless the shapes also have a stroke value (even if the stroke value is unrecognized)\"\n\nOK so the rule is: presence of a stroke attribute flips the behavior from fill to outline, regardless of whether the stroke value itself is recognized. I think the user is specifically addressing the unrecognized fill case\u2014currently those fall back to outline-only, but they should fill with the current brush instead, unless a stroke attribute is also present. So the fallback behavior needs to change: when a fill attribute exists but can't be recognized, it should fill with the current brush instead of outlining. But if there's also a stroke attribute present\u2014whether recognized or not\u2014then outline behavior takes precedence. The key insight is that the fallback should apply to fill operations, not outline operations, unless a stroke is explicitly defined.\n\nCurrently my code always routes the fallback to stroke, which is why that dark gray rect gets outlined instead of filled. I need to track whether stroke and fill attributes are actually present on the element, then apply the fallback logic based on which one exists.\n\nRefining the fallback rules... So the rule is straightforward: if there's a fill attribute, fill it; if there's a stroke attribute, outline it; if both exist, do both; if neither, fall back to the default outline. The user's phrasing about \"not outlined\u2014unless the shapes also have a stroke value\" is just clarifying that the current behavior (always outlining) is wrong, and the exception is when stroke is explicitly set, in which case outlining should happen alongside or instead of filling.\n\nNow I need to track whether fill and stroke attributes are actually present on the element, not just whether they resolve to a value. I'm thinking about modifying the paint resolution function to return that metadata alongside the resolved colors. Extending the resolver to track whether we've encountered any non-none, non-transparent stroke or fill attributes as we traverse up the tree, so I can distinguish between \"blocked by none\" and \"never saw a paint value\" for the outline behavior logic. I see the issue now \u2014 when we encounter any attribute value at a level, we stop the inheritance walk for that property, whether it's a recognized color or not. So if a rect has `fill=\"weird\"`, that blocks inheritance from parent elements even though we can't resolve \"weird\" to a paint value. The `fillSeen` flag correctly tracks that we found an attribute, and the cascade semantics work as expected: the rect's fill takes precedence over the parent's, regardless of whether we can actually interpret it.\n\nThe tricky part is the loop's break condition. Right now we exit when both stroke and fill are either resolved to paint values or explicitly blocked with \"none\", but we should also break once both properties have been *seen* at any level, since further walking up the tree won't change what we've already found.\n\nSo I need to track whether we've encountered each attribute at all, not just whether we've resolved it. If stroke was seen (even if it couldn't be resolved to a paint), we stop looking for stroke in parents. Same for fill. Let me refactor the logic to check these \"done\" states instead.\n\nRefactoring the function... Writing the fill resolution logic... Writing fallback paint logic... with fallback, stroke with rose \u2713\n\nSo the logic handles all these cases correctly\u2014when an attribute is explicitly set but unrecognized, it gets bumped to the fallback color, and the shim still routes things appropriately based on what's actually painted. with current brush, outline with rose \u2713\n\n`<rect fill=\"red\" />` gives fill with rose only \u2713\n\n`<rect stroke=\"red\" />` gives outline with rose only \u2713\n\n`<g fill=\"red\"><rect/></g>` gives fill with rose only \u2713\n\n`<g fill=\"red\"><rect stroke=\"blue\"/></g>` gives fill rose and outline blue \u2713\n\nNow I'm thinking through `<rect fill=\"none\" />` \u2014 the fill is explicitly blocked so fillSeen stays false, and with no stroke it falls back to the outline with the current brush. But I need to decide: does `fill=\"none\"` mean we should still apply a fallback stroke, or does it mean the element should be completely unstyled? In actual SVG, `fill=\"none\"` explicitly removes the fill, so a rect with only that attribute would just have no fill.\n\nI think the right approach is: `fill=\"none\"` should silence the element entirely since the user explicitly said no fill and there's no stroke. Meanwhile, `<rect />` with no attributes should fall back to outline with the current brush, and `<rect fill=\"weird\" />` should fill with the current brush since the user wanted a fill but the color wasn't recognized. Let me adjust the logic...\n\nAdjusting the condition... Handling fallback paint logic...\n\nNow I'm walking through the edge cases to verify the behavior is correct: when both fill and stroke are explicitly set to \"none\", neither gets a fallback since they're both blocked, which prevents the unstyled fallback from triggering\u2014exactly what we want.\n\nFor inheritance, I'm checking how the code handles SVG's natural cascading: when walking up the tree to find stroke or fill values, the first non-empty, non-\"none\" value sets the seen flag and stops, which correctly captures inheritance. But I need to verify the case where a child explicitly sets fill=\"none\" inside a parent with fill=\"weird\"\u2014the child's explicit \"none\" should take precedence over the parent's value. Refactoring the paint resolution logic...\n\nI realize I need to always call the resolver to detect whether fill and stroke attributes exist, even when both color flags are off. The new rule requires that if a fill attribute is present, it should trigger a fill action regardless of the resolution settings, so I can't skip the resolver entirely.\n\nNow I'm updating the fallback logic to check whether attributes were actually seen\u2014if a fill attribute existed but didn't resolve to a paint, I apply the fallback pigment instead. Same for stroke. And if neither fill nor stroke attributes were detected and neither was explicitly blocked, the stroke gets the fallback as the ultimate default.\n\nFor the backwards-compatibility path with fillShapes disabled, I'm keeping the logic that routes a resolved fill paint to stroke when there's no stroke paint already, then clears the fill\u2014that way outline mode still works as expected.\n\nNow I'm checking whether this handles the edge case correctly: when fillShapes is off and an element has a fill attribute that doesn't resolve to a real color, the fallback assigns it anyway, then gets routed to the outline. That's the right behavior\u2014it maintains v0.44 semantics where filled shapes become outlines, regardless of whether the fill color was valid or not.\n\nI should update the documentation to be clearer about what counts as a \"pure-fill\" element\u2014anything with a fill attribute, whether it's recognized or not\u2014and same for stroke elements. Then I'll start implementing this in v0.48.",

The four boolean flags describe\n// what we observed on the way up:\n//   strokeBlocked / fillBlocked \u2014 found an explicit 'none' or\n//                                 'transparent' value, which (per the\n//                                 SVG cascade) stops further inheritance\n//                                 AND tells us the author wanted no\n//                                 paint of that kind.       "description": "Replace fallback rules with attribute-presence aware version",
      "new_str": "    // v0.45 / v0.48 \u2014 Resolve stroke and fill paints independently.\n    // The resolver always runs (even with both color flags off) so we\n    // can detect attribute *presence* \u2014 see fallback block below.\n    let strokePaint = null, fillPaint = null;\n    const paints = _resolveSvgPaints(el, svg, triggerColors, approximateColor);\n    strokePaint = paints.stroke;\n    fillPaint   = paints.fill;\n\n    // v0.48 \u2014 Fallback routing now respects which attribute the SVG\n    // author actually wrote. The original rule (v0.41\u2013v0.47) always\n    // routed the fallback to the OUTLINE slot, which produced wrong\n    // results for shapes whose fill happened not to resolve \u2014 an\n    // unrecognized hex like #222222 (with approximateColor off) would\n    // get OUTLINED in the active brush when the author clearly wanted\n    // it FILLED. New rules:\n    //   - If a fill attribute was seen (recognized or not), the shape\n    //     should be FILLED. With the fill paint if it resolved; with\n    //     the active brush otherwise.\n    //   - Same for stroke.\n    //   - If both attributes were seen, both happen (fill stamps +\n    //     outline trace), each getting its own fallback as needed.\n    //   - Only when NEITHER attribute is seen ANYWHERE on the way up\n    //     (truly unstyled element) does the fallback brush trace the\n    //     outline \u2014 matching the \"trace this SVG in my active brush\"\n    //     contract from before per-attribute resolution existed.\n    //   - Explicit 'none' or 'transparent' (fillBlocked / strokeBlocked)\n    //     suppresses the corresponding paint entirely. fill=\"none\"\n    //     stroke=\"none\" produces nothing \u2014 author asked for nothing.\n    if (paints.fillSeen && !fillPaint) {\n      fillPaint = { type: 'single', pigment: fallbackPigment };\n    }\n    if (paints.strokeSeen && !strokePaint) {\n      strokePaint = { type: 'single', pigment: fallbackPigment };\n    }\n    if (!paints.fillSeen && !paints.fillBlocked &&\n        !paints.strokeSeen && !paints.strokeBlocked) {\n      // Truly unstyled \u2014 trace outline in active brush\n      strokePaint = { type: 'single', pigment: fallbackPigment };\n    }\n\n    // v0.45 \u2014 When fillShapes is off but the element has a fill, route\n    // that fill to the OUTLINE so the shape still gets painted. This\n    // preserves the v0.44 behavior (\"filled SVGs trace their outline\n    // in the fill color\") for users who opt out of fill stamping.\n    if (!fillShapes && !strokePaint && fillPaint) {\n      strokePaint = fillPaint;\n      fillPaint = null;\n    }",
      "old_str": "    // v0.45 \u2014 Resolve stroke and fill paints independently.\n    let strokePaint = null, fillPaint = null;\n    if (triggerColors || approximateColor) {\n      const paints = _resolveSvgPaints(el, svg, triggerColors, approximateColor);\n      strokePaint = paints.stroke;\n      fillPaint   = paints.fill;\n    }\n    // For backwards compatibility with the v0.41..v0.44 single-paint\n    // semantics: if neither resolved AND neither color flag is off,\n    // use the fallback as the OUTLINE paint (preserves \"trace this SVG\n    // in the active brush color\" for unstyled SVGs).\n    if (!strokePaint && !fillPaint) {\n      strokePaint = { type: 'single', pigment: fallbackPigment };\n    }\n    // v0.45 \u2014 When fillShapes is off but the element has a fill, route\n    // that fill to the OUTLINE so the shape still gets painted. This\n    // preserves the v0.44 behavior (\"filled SVGs trace their outline\n    // in the fill color\") for users who opt out of fill stamping.\n    if (!fillShapes && !strokePaint && fillPaint) {\n      strokePaint = fillPaint;\n      fillPaint = null;\n    }",

    "input": {
      "description": "Add v0.48 changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.48 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-48\">v0.48 &mdash; Fill-attribute-aware fallback for SVG</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>The author's intent now wins, even when the color doesn't resolve.</strong> Before v0.48, any element whose stroke and fill both failed to resolve (unrecognized hex with <code>approximateColor: false</code>, or both flags off entirely) had its fallback brush routed to the OUTLINE slot &mdash; so <code>&lt;rect fill=\"#222222\"&gt;</code> got outlined in the current brush even though the SVG author clearly wanted it filled. v0.48 routes the fallback to whichever slot the author specified: if there's a <code>fill</code> attribute, the shape gets filled with the active brush; if there's a <code>stroke</code> attribute, it gets outlined; if both, both happen.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>New flags exposed by the resolver:</strong> <code>strokeSeen</code> / <code>fillSeen</code> on the return value of <code>_resolveSvgPaints</code>. These flip to <code>true</code> whenever a non-<code>none</code> stroke or fill value is found anywhere on the way up the DOM tree, regardless of whether the value resolves to a recognized paint. <code>strokeBlocked</code> / <code>fillBlocked</code> remain &mdash; they specifically mark <code>none</code> / <code>transparent</code> values that suppress paint entirely.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>SVG cascade now matches CSS:</strong> a child's explicit attribute (recognized or not) takes precedence over a parent's. Before, an unrecognized child stroke would keep walking up looking for a recognized one; now it stops at the first non-<code>none</code> value, matching how browsers render SVG.</li>\n          <li><strong>Behavior matrix</strong> (active brush = current pigment when fallback is used):\n            <ul>\n              <li><code>&lt;rect/&gt;</code> (truly unstyled) &rarr; outline with active brush (unchanged)</li>\n              <li><code>fill=\"#FF0000\"</code> &rarr; fill rose only (unchanged)</li>\n              <li><code>stroke=\"#FF0000\"</code> &rarr; outline rose only (unchanged)</li>\n              <li><code>fill=\"#222222\"</code> (unrecognized) &rarr; <strong>fill with active brush</strong> (was: outline with active brush)</li>\n              <li><code>stroke=\"#222222\"</code> (unrecognized) &rarr; outline with active brush (unchanged)</li>\n              <li><code>fill=\"#FF0000\" stroke=\"#222222\"</code> &rarr; fill rose AND outline with active brush</li>\n              <li><code>fill=\"#222222\" stroke=\"#FF0000\"</code> &rarr; fill with active brush AND outline rose</li>\n              <li><code>fill=\"none\"</code> &rarr; nothing (explicit suppression respected)</li>\n              <li><code>fill=\"none\" stroke=\"#FF0000\"</code> &rarr; outline rose only</li>\n            </ul>\n          </li>\n          <li>All v0.47 spacing improvements preserved. All v0.45/v0.46 fill stamping + instant fill behavior preserved for recognized colors.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.47 ===== -->",

    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-12T07:03:31.751746Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.48 shipped.** The author's intent now governs where the fallback goes.\n\n## The conceptual fix\n\nBefore v0.48, the SVG paint resolver only cared whether colors *resolved*. The fallback brush always went to the outline slot, on the assumption that \"trace this in my active brush\" is the most useful default. That assumption broke for filled shapes whose colors don't happen to be triggers \u2014 a graphic with `fill=\"#2a2a2a\"` (off-black, common in icon sets) got *outlined* in the user's brush, even though the author wrote a fill attribute that clearly meant \"fill this region.\"\n\nv0.48 distinguishes two questions:\n\n1. **Did the color resolve to a recognized paint?** (existing logic \u2014 trigger or approximate)\n2. **Did the author write the attribute at all?** (new \u2014 tracked via `strokeSeen` / `fillSeen` flags)\n\nThe fallback brush then goes to whichever slot the *author* specified. If you wrote `fill=`, you get a fill (with the resolved color, or the active brush if unresolved). If you wrote `stroke=`, you get an outline. Both attributes present \u2192 both happen. Neither attribute \u2192 outline with active brush (the unstyled-SVG case, unchanged).\n\n## Behavior matrix\n\nThe complete table, with `fallback = active brush`:\n\n| SVG | Result |\n|---|---|\n| `<rect/>` | outline with fallback (unchanged) |\n| `fill=\"#FF0000\"` | fill rose only (unchanged) |\n| `stroke=\"#FF0000\"` | outline rose only (unchanged) |\n| `fill=\"#FF0000\" stroke=\"#FFFF00\"` | fill rose + outline yellow (unchanged) |\n| **`fill=\"#222222\"` (unrecognized)** | **fill with fallback** \u2190 was: outline with fallback |\n| `stroke=\"#222222\"` (unrecognized) | outline with fallback (unchanged) |\n| `fill=\"#222\" stroke=\"#333\"` | fill fallback + outline fallback |\n| `fill=\"#FF0000\" stroke=\"#333\"` | fill rose + outline fallback |\n| `fill=\"#333\" stroke=\"#FF0000\"` | fill fallback + outline rose |\n| `fill=\"none\"` | nothing (explicit suppression) |\n| `fill=\"none\" stroke=\"#FF0000\"` | outline rose only |\n| `fill=\"#FF0000\" stroke=\"none\"` | fill rose only |\n\nAll 12 cases verified end-to-end with stamp counts matching expectations.\n\n## Adjacent behavior change\n\nI also tightened the SVG cascade to match how browsers actually render SVG: a child's explicit attribute (recognized or not) takes precedence over a parent's. Before v0.48, an unrecognized child `stroke=` would keep walking up looking for a recognized parent value. Now it stops at the child \u2014 matching CSS/SVG cascade rules.\n\nSo `<g stroke=\"red\"><rect stroke=\"weird\"/></g>` now produces an outline in the active brush (rect's `stroke` won), not in rose (which v0.47 would have inherited from `g`). The rect explicitly specified a stroke value; the author meant something even if we couldn't parse it.\n\n## Try it\n\nLoad any SVG with explicit fill colors. Even with `triggerColors: true, approximateColor: false` (the default), shapes with unrecognized fills now paint as filled regions in the active brush color instead of skeletal outlines. The most visible difference: icon-set SVGs and dark-gray illustrations are now usable without flipping on `approximateColor`.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

Elements with a stroke also get their outline traced as before; filled+stroked shapes get both. When <code>false</code>, the v0.44 behavior is preserved: filled-only shapes have their outline traced in the fill color (which is invisible for shapes without an explicit stroke). See \\\"Filled shape stamping\\\" below.</dd>\\n1983:          <dd>When <code>true</code> (default), fill stamps paint <strong>synchronously</strong> at load time instead of animating through the trace queue. The filled region appears as a wash the moment <code>traceSVG()</code> is called, and any outlines (if present) animate separately over <code>durationMs</code>. When <code>false</code>, fills animate point-by-point through the bounding-box grid alongside outlines (the v0.45 behavior, which reads as a sweep across the shape). Has no effect on stroke-only SVGs.         "text": "  2148\t        <h3>Filled shape stamping (v0.45+)</h3>\n  2149\t        <p>\n  2150\t          Before v0.45 the SVG loader only traced <strong>outlines</strong>\n  2151\t          &mdash; it sampled points along each element's perimeter and\n  2152\t          painted them in order. Stroked SVGs (line drawings, contours,\n  2153\t          calligraphic strokes) worked beautifully. Filled SVGs (logos,\n  2154\t          icons, regions with solid colors and no stroke) were\n  2155\t          effectively invisible: the loader traced the outline using\n  2156\t          the fill color, but a one-pixel-thick outline doesn't read as\n  2157\t          \"a filled red square.\"\n  2158\t        </p>\n  2159\t        <p>\n  2160\t          v0.45 changes that. <code>fillShapes: true</code> (default)\n  2161\t          adds a <strong>fill pass</strong> alongside the outline pass.\n  2162\t          For each element with a recognized fill paint, the loader:\n  2163\t        </p>\n  2164\t        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n  2165\t          <li>Computes the element's bounding box via <code>p.getBBox()</code></li>\n  2166\t          <li>Samples a regular grid of points across the bounding box, spaced at <code>0.85 &times; brushRadius</code> in grid units (tight, slightly overlapping coverage)</li>\n  2167\t          <li>For each sample, calls <code>p.isPointInFill({x, y})</code> &mdash; the browser's exact fill-test, including holes via the fill-rule</li>\n  2168\t          <li>Keeps the inside points and adds them to the trace list with the fill paint</li>\n  2169\t        </ol>\n  2170\t        <p>\n  2171\t          The fill samples come <strong>before</strong> outline samples\n  2172\t          in the array, so when both are present the outline appears on\n  2173\t          top of the fill &mdash; the natural watercolor \"wash first,\n  2174\t          ink the edge after\" order. Filled+stroked elements get both.\n  2175\t          Pure-fill elements just get stamped. Pure-stroke elements\n  2176\t          just get outlined (unchanged from v0.44).\n  2177\t        </p>\n  2178\t        <p>\n  2179\t          <strong>Stroke and fill resolve independently.</strong> Before\n  2180\t          v0.45 the resolver collapsed both to a single paint: it walked\n  2181\t          looking for any recognized stroke OR fill and used the first\n  2182\t          hit. v0.45 splits them. An element with <code>stroke=\"blue\"\n  2183\t          fill=\"yellow\"</code> resolves to {stroke: blue, fill: yellow}\n  2184\t          &mdash; the interior stamps in yellow and the outline traces\n  2185\t          in blue. <code>fill=\"none\"</code> explicitly blocks fill\n  2186\t          inheritance up the tree; <code>stroke=\"none\"</code> blocks\n  2187\t          stroke inheritance.\n  2188\t        </p>\n  2189\t        <p>\n  2190\t          <strong>Sample density &amp; cap.</strong> Spacing of\n  2191\t          <code>0.85 &times; brushRadius</code> means roughly 1.4 stamps\n  2192\t          per brush diameter. Dense enough for solid coverage with\n  2193\t          natural watercolor variation, sparse enough to avoid\n  2194\t          oversampling. There's a 10000-sample hard cap per element to\n  2195\t          protect against pathological SVGs (huge viewBoxes + tiny\n  2196\t          brush). Hitting the cap silently skips the fill for that\n  2197\t          element and continues with the outline.\n  2198\t        </p>\n  2199\t        <p>\n  2200\t          <strong>Wetness consequence.</strong> A large filled shape\n  2201\t          fires hundreds to thousands of <code>paintAt</code> calls in\n  2202\t          its bounding box. Combined with <code>approximateColor</code>,\n  2203\t          each can become 2&ndash;3 calls per stamp (one per pigment\n  2204\t          weight). The simulation handles this fine, but if you're\n  2205\t          stamping a wide solid wash of a dark color, expect a lot of\n  2206\t          wet pigment migration during the trace. <code>paperWetness('dry')</code>\n  2207\t          before the trace gives crisper edges; <code>paperWetness('wet')</code>\n  2208\t          beforehand encourages dramatic bleeding.\n  2209\t        </p>\n  2210\t        <p>\n  2211\t          <strong>Off-switch.</strong> <code>fillShapes: false</code>\n  2212\t          falls back to the v0.44 behavior: if an element has a fill\n  2213\t          but no stroke, the outline gets traced in the fill color\n  2214\t          (which is invisible for typical filled SVGs &mdash; use this\n  2215\t          flag only if you specifically want the old behavior). Filled+\n  2216\t          stroked elements still trace just the outline in this mode.\n  2217\t        </p>\n  2218\t        <p>\n  2219\t          <strong>Instant fills (v0.46+).</strong> By default, fill\n  2220\t          stamps paint <strong>synchronously</strong> the moment\n  2221\t          <code>traceSVG()</code> is called &mdash; the entire wash\n  2222\t          appears in one frame rather than animating across the\n  2223\t          duration. This is what the brain expects from a watercolor\n  2224\t          wash: you push a wet brush onto paper and the whole footprint\n  2225\t          deposits at once. The trace animation is reserved for\n  2226\t          <em>outlines</em>, which actually benefit from sequential\n  2227\t          reveal (a pen-stroke really does follow a path).\n  2228\t        </p>\n  2229\t        <p>\n  2230\t          Without instant fills, v0.45 painted the bounding-box grid in\n  2231\t          row-major order, which the eye read as \"the brush is sweeping\n  2232\t          across the shape\" &mdash; still better than tracing the\n  2233\t          perimeter, but not what a watercolorist sees. With instant\n  2234\t          fills, you get the wash-then-ink rhythm: filled regions land\n  2235\t          immediately, outlines animate over them. Pass\n  2236\t          <code>fillInstant: false</code> to restore the v0.45\n  2237\t          row-by-row sweep behavior.\n  2238\t        </p>\n  2239\t        <p>\n  2240\t          <strong>Performance note.</strong> Instant fills can fire\n  2241\t          hundreds to thousands of <code>paintAt</code> calls in a\n  2242\t          single frame for a large SVG. The simulation handles this\n  2243\t          fine on desktop hardware; on mobile or for very dense SVGs\n  2244\t          you might see a brief stutter on the first frame. If that's\n  2245\t          a concern, <code>fillInstant: false</code> spreads the work\n  2246\t          over <code>durationMs</code> at the cost of the sweep look.\n  2247\t        </p>\n  2248\t\n  2249\t        <h3>Easing curves for drawing animation</h3>\n  2250\t        <p>\n[10148 lines total]",

Pure-stroke elements\\n          just get outlined (unchanged from v0.44).\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Stroke and fill resolve independently.</strong> Before\\n          v0.45 the resolver collapsed both to a single paint: it walked\\n          looking for any recognized stroke OR fill and used the first\\n          hit. v0.45 splits them. An element with <code>stroke=\\\"blue\\\"\\n          fill=\\\"yellow\\\"</code> resolves to {stroke: blue, fill: yellow}\\n          &mdash; the interior stamps in yellow and the outline traces\\n          in blue. <code>fill=\\\"none\\\"</code> explicitly blocks fill\\n          inheritance up the tree; <code>stroke=\\\"none\\\"</code> blocks\\n          stroke inheritance.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Sample density &amp; cap.</strong> Spacing of\\n          <code>0.85 &times; brushRadius</code> means roughly 1.4 stamps\\n          per brush diameter. Dense enough for solid coverage with\\n          natural watercolor variation, sparse enough to avoid\\n          oversampling. There's a 10000-sample hard cap per element to\\n          protect against pathological SVGs (huge viewBoxes + tiny\\n          brush). Hitting the cap silently skips the fill for that\\n          element and continues with the outline.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Wetness consequence.</strong> A large filled shape\\n          fires hundreds to thousands of <code>paintAt</code> calls in\\n          its bounding box. Combined with <code>approximateColor</code>,\\n          each can become 2&ndash;3 calls per stamp (one per pigment\\n          weight). The simulation handles this fine, but if you're\\n          stamping a wide solid wash of a dark color, expect a lot of\\n          wet pigment migration during the trace. <code>paperWetness('dry')</code>\\n          before the trace gives crisper edges; <code>paperWetness('wet')</code>\\n          beforehand encourages dramatic bleeding.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Off-switch.</strong> <code>fillShapes: false</code>\\n          falls back to the v0.44 behavior: if an element has a fill\\n          but no stroke, the outline gets traced in the fill color\\n          (which is invisible for typical filled SVGs &mdash; use this\\n          flag only if you specifically want the old behavior). Filled+\\n          stroked elements still trace just the outline in this mode.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Instant fills (v0.46+).</strong> By default, fill\\n          stamps paint <strong>synchronously</strong> the moment\\n          <code>traceSVG()</code> is called &mdash; the entire wash\\n          appears in one frame rather than animating across the\\n          duration. This is what the brain expects from a watercolor\\n          wash: you push a wet brush onto paper and the whole footprint\\n          deposits at once. The trace animation is reserved for\\n          <em>outlines</em>, which actually benefit from sequential\\n          reveal (a pen-stroke really does follow a path).\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          Without instant fills, v0.45 painted the bounding-box grid in\\n          row-major order, which the eye read as \\\"the brush is sweeping\\n          across the shape\\\" &mdash; still better than tracing the\\n          perimeter, but not what a watercolorist sees. With instant\\n          fills, you get the wash-then-ink rhythm: filled regions land\\n          immediately, outlines animate over them. Pass\\n          <code>fillInstant: false</code> to restore the v0.45\\n          row-by-row sweep behavior.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Performance note.</strong> Instant fills can fire\\n          hundreds to thousands of <code>paintAt</code> calls in a\\n          single frame for a large SVG.       "description": "Add v0.48 fallback rules subsection",
      "new_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Stroke and fill resolve independently.</strong> Before\n          v0.45 the resolver collapsed both to a single paint: it walked\n          looking for any recognized stroke OR fill and used the first\n          hit. v0.45 splits them. An element with <code>stroke=\"blue\"\n          fill=\"yellow\"</code> resolves to {stroke: blue, fill: yellow}\n          &mdash; the interior stamps in yellow and the outline traces\n          in blue. <code>fill=\"none\"</code> explicitly blocks fill\n          inheritance up the tree; <code>stroke=\"none\"</code> blocks\n          stroke inheritance.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Fallback routing for unrecognized colors (v0.48+)</h3>\n        <p>\n          When a fill or stroke value doesn't resolve to a recognized\n          paint &mdash; an unrecognized hex with <code>approximateColor: false</code>,\n          a CSS color name not in the parser's table, both flags off\n          entirely &mdash; the loader has to choose what to do with the\n          active brush. The rule (v0.48+) is to respect which\n          <strong>attribute</strong> the author actually wrote, not\n          just whether it parsed:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Author wrote <code>fill=\"...\"</code> and it didn't resolve &rarr; the shape gets <strong>filled with the active brush</strong>. The author asked for a fill; we don't know the color, so we use yours.</li>\n          <li>Author wrote <code>stroke=\"...\"</code> and it didn't resolve &rarr; <strong>outline with the active brush</strong>. Same reasoning.</li>\n          <li>Author wrote both &rarr; both happen, each falling back as needed (recognized values keep their resolved paints; unrecognized values use the active brush).</li>\n          <li>Author wrote neither &rarr; <strong>outline with the active brush</strong>. The \"trace this SVG in my active brush\" contract from the earliest versions.</li>\n          <li>Author wrote <code>fill=\"none\"</code> or <code>stroke=\"none\"</code> &rarr; that paint is suppressed entirely. No fallback. The author explicitly said no.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Before v0.48, the fallback brush always went to the OUTLINE\n          slot regardless of which attribute the author wrote &mdash;\n          so a graphic with <code>fill=\"#2a2a2a\"</code> (off-black,\n          common in icon sets) got outlined in the active brush instead\n          of filled, leaving a hollow shape on the canvas. v0.48 routes\n          the fallback to the slot the author asked for, which makes\n          most icon-style SVGs work the way the eye expects them to.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Adjacent change:</strong> the SVG cascade now matches\n          how browsers actually render SVG. A child's explicit attribute\n          (recognized or not) takes precedence over a parent's. Before\n          v0.48 an unrecognized child <code>stroke=</code> would keep\n          walking up looking for a recognized parent value; now it\n          stops at the child. <code>&lt;g stroke=\"red\"&gt;&lt;rect\n          stroke=\"weird\"/&gt;&lt;/g&gt;</code> now outlines the rect\n          in the active brush (rect's stroke won, the value didn't\n          resolve, fallback applies), not in rose (which v0.47 would\n          have inherited from g).\n        </p>\n",

Default: <code>pigment()</code>.</dd>\\n1977:          <dd>When <code>true</code> (default), recognized fill/stroke colors on each SVG element route that element's strokes to the matching pigment via strict chroma-key matching (see table below). Set <code>false</code> to disable trigger-color matching entirely. The old <code>colorMap</code> name still works as an alias. With v0.48+, unrecognized colors don't fall back to the brush <em>and lose their fill/outline role</em> &mdash; they keep their role (fill or stroke) and just use the active brush as the color. See \\\"Fallback routing\\\" below.</dd>\\n1979:          <dd>When <code>true</code>, parseable colors that aren't exact triggers get decomposed into a mix of rose+yellow+blue and painted as such &mdash; e.g. <code>#FF8000</code> orange becomes ~35% rose + 100% yellow, <code>#808080</code> grey becomes equal parts of all three. Default <code>false</code>. Can be combined with <code>triggerColors</code>: triggers win for exact matches, approximation handles everything else. With v0.48+, colors that don't parse (HSL, unknown names, etc.) still respect their author-specified role &mdash; <code>fill=\\\"hsl(...)\\\"</code> fills with the active brush, <code>stroke=\\\"hsl(...)\\\"</code> outlines. See \\\"Approximated color mixing\\\" below for the algorithm.</dd>\\n1981:          <dd>When <code>true</code> (default), elements with a recognized fill paint get their <strong>interior stamped</strong> &mdash; a regular grid of sample points inside the path, each painted with the fill paint. Elements with a stroke also get their outline traced as before; filled+stroked shapes get both. When <code>false</code>, the v0.44 behavior is preserved: filled-only shapes have their outline traced in the fill color (which is invisible for shapes without an explicit stroke). See \\\"Filled shape stamping\\\" below.</dd>\\n1983:          <dd>When <code>true</code> (default), fill stamps paint <strong>synchronously</strong> at load time instead of animating through the trace queue. The filled region appears as a wash the moment <code>traceSVG()</code> is called, and any outlines (if present) animate separately over <code>durationMs</code>. When <code>false</code>, fills animate point-by-point through the bounding-box grid alongside outlines (the v0.45 behavior, which reads as a sweep across the shape). Has no effect on stroke-only SVGs. See \\\"Filled shape stamping\\\" below for why this matters.</dd>\\n2028:          <li>Final fallback: <code>currentPigment</code></li>\\n2065:          that way. See \\\"Fallback routing\\\" below.</dd>\\n1979:          <dd>When <code>true</code>, parseable colors that aren't exact triggers get decomposed into a mix of rose+yellow+blue and painted as such &mdash; e.g. <code>#FF8000</code> orange becomes ~35% rose + 100% yellow, <code>#808080</code> grey becomes equal parts of all three. With v0.48+, colors that don't parse (HSL, unknown names, etc.) still respect their author-specified role &mdash; <code>fill=\\\"hsl(...)\\\"</code> fills with the active brush, <code>stroke=\\\"hsl(...)\\\"</code> outlines. See \\\"Approximated color mixing\\\" below for the algorithm.</dd>\\n1981:          <dd>When <code>true</code> (default), elements with a recognized fill paint get their <strong>interior stamped</strong> &mdash; a regular grid of sample points inside the path, each painted with the fill paint. Elements with a stroke also get their outline traced as before; filled+stroked shapes get both. When <code>false</code>, the v0.44 behavior is preserved: filled-only shapes have their outline traced in the fill color (which is invisible for shapes without an explicit stroke). See \\\"Filled shape stamping\\\" below.</dd>\\n1983:          <dd>When <code>true</code> (default), fill stamps paint <strong>synchronously</strong> at load time instead of animating through the trace queue. The filled region appears as a wash the moment <code>traceSVG()</code> is called, and any outlines (if present) animate separately over <code>durationMs</code>. When <code>false</code>, fills animate point-by-point through the bounding-box grid alongside outlines (the v0.45 behavior, which reads as a sweep across the shape). Has no effect on stroke-only SVGs. See \\\"Filled shape stamping\\\" below for why this matters.</dd>\\n2028:          <li>Final fallback: <code>currentPigment</code></li>\\n2065:          that way. Set <code>false</code> to disable trigger-color matching entirely. The old <code>colorMap</code> name still works as an alias. With v0.48+, unrecognized colors don't fall back to the brush <em>and lose their fill/outline role</em> &mdash; they keep their role (fill or stroke) and just use the active brush as the color.       "description": "Update resolution order to v0.45+ independent semantics",
      "new_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Resolution model (v0.45+):</strong> stroke and fill\n          resolve <em>independently</em>. The loader walks up the DOM\n          tree separately for each property, collecting the first\n          non-<code>none</code> value it finds at each level. Each\n          property contributes its own pass to the canvas &mdash; a\n          fill paint stamps the interior, a stroke paint traces the\n          outline, and an element with both gets both.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          For each property the walk goes:\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Element's own <code>stroke=</code> / <code>fill=</code> attribute (or inline <code>style</code>)</li>\n          <li>If absent, walk up through parent <code>&lt;g&gt;</code> groups, taking the first attribute found</li>\n          <li>Stop at the <code>&lt;svg&gt;</code> root</li>\n          <li>Resolve the found value to a paint via <code>triggerColors</code> (strict) then <code>approximateColor</code> (CMY mix)</li>\n          <li>If still unresolved but the attribute was <em>present</em> somewhere, fall back to <code>opts.pigment</code> (or <code>currentPigment</code>) in that slot &mdash; see v0.48 fallback rules below</li>\n        </ol>\n        <p>\n          An explicit <code>fill=\"none\"</code> or <code>stroke=\"none\"</code>\n          stops the walk and suppresses that paint entirely. No\n          fallback is applied for explicit <code>none</code> values\n          &mdash; the author asked for nothing.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Worked example:</strong> a single SVG drives three\n          pigments in one call:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// orchard-sketch.svg\n&lt;svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\"&gt;\n  &lt;path d=\"M10 80 Q50 20 90 80\"  stroke=\"#FF0000\" fill=\"none\" /&gt;\n  &lt;circle cx=\"50\" cy=\"50\" r=\"20\" stroke=\"#FFFF00\" fill=\"none\" /&gt;\n  &lt;line x1=\"0\" y1=\"100\" x2=\"100\" y2=\"100\" stroke=\"#0000FF\" /&gt;\n&lt;/svg&gt;\n\n// JS \u2014 no per-element wiring needed\nwc.traceSVG(svgText, { durationMs: 1500, easing: 'penStroke' });\n// curve traces in rose, circle in yellow, baseline in blue</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Backwards compatibility:</strong> the chroma-key\n          matching is strict, so existing SVGs that use black strokes,\n          neutral fills, or arbitrary colors don't trip the trigger\n          path &mdash; they fall through to <code>opts.pigment</code>\n          via the v0.48+ fallback rules (filled elements get filled\n          with it, stroked elements outlined). For a paranoid escape\n          hatch (e.g. you have an SVG that happens to use these pure\n          primaries for unrelated reasons), pass\n          <code>triggerColors: false</code>.",
      "old_str": "        <p>\n          <strong>Resolution order:</strong>\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Element's <code>stroke</code> attribute or inline <code>style</code> stroke</li>\n          <li>Element's <code>fill</code> attribute or inline <code>style</code> fill</li>\n          <li>Parent <code>&lt;g&gt;</code>'s stroke, then fill (walks up to <code>&lt;svg&gt;</code> root)</li>\n          <li>Fallback: <code>opts.pigment</code></li>\n          <li>Final fallback: <code>currentPigment</code></li>\n        </ol>\n        <p>\n          Stroke takes precedence because most SVG \"sketches\" used\n          here are line drawings where the visible color is in the\n          stroke.
